building:102024695.desc: Built under the Carian satrap Mausolus in the 4th century BC and enlarged in the 2nd century AD under Rome, this theater held 10,000-13,000 spectators; a nearby platform was once mistaken for a temple of Ares.

building:107080283.desc: A small Roman-era theater (likely an odeon for music and recitation) at Antipatris, the city Herod the Great rebuilt around 9 BCE; it may never have been finished or was heavily stripped of stone before excavation.

building:139971323.desc: Excavated in 1829-1830 at the Roman colony of Falerii Novi, this rock-cut theater (compared to the amphitheater at Sutri) yielded Augustan-era statues of Livia and her grandsons Gaius and Lucius Caesar; little survives above ground today.

building:150310592.desc: Discovered in 1985 at the Gallo-Roman vicus of Ricciacum, this 2nd-century AD theater seated about 3,500 and is notable for rare surviving 'honor seats' with backrests and armrests, unique among known theaters in Gaul.

building:150531239.desc: A 2nd-century BC theater roughly 165 feet in diameter at the Lycian city of Cyaneae (Kyaneai), considered the best-preserved structure at the site, which also has an extensive rock-cut necropolis.

building:151530992.desc: A late-3rd/2nd-century BC theater at the foot of the acropolis of Iuvanum, a Samnite-then-Roman town in Abruzzo; only the lowest seven rows of seating and part of the orchestra are still visible.

building:154948553.desc: The rock-cut cavea of a theater, roughly 115 meters across, was identified in 1997 on the Zegreg Tepe hillside at Tavium, capital of the Galatian Trocmi tribe and a Roman-era crossroads town.

building:177668735.desc: Small deme theater at Ikarion, cult center of the Rural Dionysia and reputed home of Thespis, traditionally credited with inventing tragedy; excavated in 1888-89 by the American School, revealing stone proedria seats beside the orchestra.

building:184642281.desc: A roughly 2,000-seat theater built for the Pausilypon villa of the Roman knight Vedius Pollio near Naples; on his death in 15 BC the estate passed to Augustus and remained an imperial retreat for his successors.

building:199686145.desc: Roman theater of the imperial-era colony of Privernum (modern Priverno), part of a marble-decorated public complex with statues and portraits of the imperial family; known mainly from excavation and epigraphic finds rather than literary sources.

building:204612618.desc: Roman-period theater at Tios (Tieion) on the Black Sea coast, judged in the late 19th century among the best-preserved in Asia Minor with an estimated capacity of about 7,000; quarried for building stone before excavations began in 2006.

building:221152517.desc: Small Roman theater at Adraha (modern Daraa, Syria), part of a classical quarter south of the Ottoman-era town alongside a colonnaded decumanus and a temple; known chiefly from surface ruins rather than formal excavation.

building:246534016.desc: Roman theater of Forum Iulii (Fréjus), rebuilt in stone under Tiberius on the site of an earlier wooden theater, with an original capacity of about 2,000; restored and returned to performance use in 1997.

building:248782117.desc: One of five known theaters at Cyrene, on the acropolis ridge; a minor, conventionally numbered structure distinct from the much larger Greek Theatre (Theatre 1) near the Sanctuary of Apollo, with little published individual documentation.

building:24962619.desc: Well-preserved 2nd-3rd century Roman theater at Thubursicum Numidarum (Khamissa, Algeria), considered one of the finest in North Africa, with a 56.8 m cavea and 43.6 m stage front; first excavated by French scholars, 1900-1922.

building:285468794.desc: Roman theater at the coastal colony of Castrum Novum, built in the Imperial age facing the sea, with a ~30 m stage and seating for roughly 1,000; brick stamps of Marcus Aurelius indicate 2nd-century restructuring, confirmed in excavations resumed since 2010.

building:293222275.desc: Mud-brick Greek theater built for Babylon's Greek community, probably dating to Alexander the Great's reign and rebuilt in the 2nd century AD per a Greek repair inscription; excavated by Robert Koldewey, final diameter about 24.8 m.

building:295344387.desc: A circular assembly hall (ekklesiasterion) unique in the Greek world, dominating Metapontum's agora and seating up to 7,500-8,000; converted into a theater around 325-300 BC, later repaired, then sealed up as a fortress.

building:339848370.desc: Roman-era odeon/small theater at ancient Kelenderis (Aydıncık), first traced from a surviving semicircular diazoma wall; excavations from 1989 and documentation since 2020 have exposed its cavea, orchestra, and stage.

building:364392645.desc: Monumental Gallo-Roman theater built around 120 AD east of Samarobriva (Amiens), with a 132 m facade ranking among Gaul's largest and estimated capacity over 5,000; unexpectedly discovered in 2006 excavations.

building:368305524.desc: A circular orchestra in the Athenian Agora used for dramatic and musical contests before the Theatre of Dionysus existed; a replacement set of markers was made by sculptors Kritios and Nesiotes in 476 BC.

building:372021190.desc: Stone-built theater of Carthaea (Karthaia) on Kea, dated to the 4th century BC and seating nearly 1,000; first examined by Danish antiquarian Peter Oluf Brønsted in 1812, later excavated and restored 2011-2015.

building:427981474.desc: Roman theater of Augusta Bagiennorum (Bene Vagienna), 1st century AD, with a cavea about 57 m across holding ~3,000 spectators; decorated with Luni marble and imported colored marbles, identified in the late 19th century and restored in the 1950s.

building:436809109.desc: Theater of the Roman colony Colonia Genetiva Iulia (Urso), founded by Julius Caesar for army veterans in 44 BC; its cavea rests against a natural hillside and has been documented by scholars since the 1870s.

building:448119705.desc: Theater of the Roman municipium of Singilia Barba near Antequera, with a cavea about 52 m across; known since the 16th century, its upper seating tiers were later quarried to build Antequera's Church of San Juan de Dios.

building:458747378.desc: Theater of ancient Telmessos (modern Fethiye), begun in the late Hellenistic or Augustan period with its stage added around 200 AD; excavated 1992-95, it held about 6,000 spectators across 28 rows.

building:48637573.desc: Roman theater at ancient Soli, Cyprus, dated by coin finds to the mid-1st century AD and used into the 4th century; excavated in 1927 by the Swedish Cyprus Expedition under Alfred Westholm and rebuilt in 1962-64, it seated about 3,500.

building:511519022.desc: Theater at Noviodunum Diablintum (Jublains), built over an earlier structure probably in the later 1st century AD; unusually elliptical and lacking a stage wall, it was donated to the town by the decurion Orgetorix per a surviving inscription.

building:523291014.desc: Theater of ancient Rhodiapolis in Lycia, with 16 surviving rows of seating; it stood before the funerary monument of Opramoas, the city's wealthy 2nd-century AD benefactor, and was fully excavated after 2006.

building:544710174.desc: Theater of the mountaintop Pisidian city Termessos, built in the Hellenistic 2nd century BC and remodeled by the Romans in the 2nd century AD; its diazoma-divided cavea seated 4,000-5,000, with animal pens beneath the stage for beast shows.

building:55405152.desc: Theater of ancient Tralles (Aydin), birthplace of Anthemius, architect of Hagia Sophia; the ruins, now on private farmland, are noted for the storks nesting atop them.

building:575107588.desc: Greek theatre built in the late 3rd century BC under Hiero II of Syracuse, with nine wedge-shaped seating sections and twelve rows holding roughly 700 spectators; still hosts performances today.

building:585690561.desc: Built under Augustus (late 1st century BC), Mediolanum's theatre had a 95m-wide cavea and 20m stage, holding an estimated 7,000-9,000 spectators; its remains lie beneath the Palazzo Turati.

building:612060178.desc: A 2nd-century BC theatre, among the last built in Greek style in Calabria, with a freestanding stone cavea originally holding around 1,200 spectators; discovered in 1883, fully excavated by 1925.

building:622079673.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Phoinike in Epirus, built mid-3rd century BC and renovated in the 2nd, seating roughly 7,000 with a 30m scaenae frons; remained in use after Rome annexed Epirus under Augustus.

building:625262900.desc: Built by Tigranes the Great around 69 BC for Greek drama performed by artists gathered from across his empire — the first known theatre in Armenia; still unfinished when Lucullus took the city and celebrated his triumph there.

building:69311541.desc: Roman theatre at Aquinum on the Via Latina in the Liri valley, birthplace of the poet Juvenal; excavations exposed 22 radial cavea walls in reticulate-faced concrete plus part of the stage and orchestra steps.

building:694724623.desc: Chance find in 1929 in the gardens of Palazzo Romano; built in the Augustan period, its six-wedge cavea (75m diameter) held some 5,000-6,000 spectators for comedies and tragedies, with later Antonine-era statuary added.

building:697765430.desc: Carved into a hillside outside Cuicul's walls, this two-tier theater seated about 3,000; a surviving inscription naming patrons Caius Julius Crescens and Caius Julius Didius Crescentianus dates its dedication to AD 161.

building:702050141.desc: Excavated in 1967 south of the Gosbecks Romano-Celtic temple near Camulodunum; an earthwork structure rebuilt in stone-revetted turf and clay, estimated to have seated up to 5,000 - the largest of Britain's five known Roman theaters.

building:702770983.desc: Theater of Roman Albintimilium, built roughly late 2nd-early 3rd century AD with its cavea partly cut into bedrock, holding about 2,000 spectators; systematically excavated from the 1930s onward.

building:713959974.desc: A Greek theater at Parium on the Hellespont, rebuilt by the Romans in the 2nd and early 3rd centuries AD; the site has been under renewed archaeological excavation since 2005.

building:716466643.desc: The oldest theater in Roman Africa, dedicated in AD 1-2 and partly financed by local benefactor Annobal Tapapius Rufus (who also built the city market); cut into a hillside once used as a cemetery, second in size in Africa only to Sabratha's theater.

building:717575054.desc: One of two theaters on the Mont Châtelet site near Breteuil (Oise); this smaller theater dates to the 1st century AD, while the adjacent grand theater (late 1st-early 2nd century) held about 4,000. Located via 1955 aerial photography and excavated 1956-1986.

building:727686421.desc: A well-preserved theater on the slopes of ancient Arykanda in Lycia, built around the mid-1st century BC with 20 rows of seats in seven wedges; its stage building was remodeled in the late 2nd-early 3rd century AD.

building:756231346.desc: The Theatre of Balbus, Rome's third public theatre, was built in 13 BC by Lucius Cornelius Balbus the Younger from spoils of his African campaign; its rear portico, excavated from 1981, gives the site its modern name Crypta Balbi.

building:763220261.desc: Hellenistic theatre (2nd century BC, with Roman-era alterations) at Laodicea on the Lycus, cut directly into bedrock and seating roughly 8,000-10,000; damaged by earthquakes in the 3rd century and in 368 AD, it was excavated and restored 2003-2021.

building:786052705.desc: Theatre of Stratos, capital of the Acarnanian League, begun in the late 4th century BC as a stoa-like stage building; enlarged through three phases into the 2nd century BC, it held some 6,000-7,000 spectators and is the largest theatre known in Acarnania.

building:832363479.desc: Built at the sacred festival site of Dion probably under Philip V (221-179 BC), this Hellenistic theatre hosted the Macedonian kings' Olympia games; tradition holds that Euripides's Archelaus and Bacchae, written during his stay at the Macedonian court, were staged there.

building:836258799.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Magarsus, the sea-facing sanctuary port of Mallos in Cilicia where Alexander the Great reportedly sacrificed to Athena Magarsis before Issus; excavated from 2013, it seated about 3,000 and is noted as the only ancient theatre with a sea view.

building:856482651.desc: Roman theatre of ancient Hatria, built in the 1st century AD under Augustus with a cavea about 70m across and capacity near 10,000; its remains were rediscovered in 1993 during demolition of a former orphanage building.

building:865005128.desc: Theatre of Gallo-Roman Decempagi, revealed only by aerial photography in 1981; an unusually large flat-ground cavea roughly 117m in diameter, estimated to have held up to 16,000, forming part of a major sanctuary complex at the site.

building:891801761.desc: Not a theater: this is the open courtyard (about 30x40m) northwest of the Minoan palace at Malia, which substituted for a formal theatral area since the flat terrain precluded one; its perimeter foundations once carried tiered seating for spectators.

building:899795549.desc: Known as the South Theater, this odeon at Hadrian's Villa seated about 1,100 in a 55m marble-and-mosaic cavea; connected to the rest of the imperial complex by underground service tunnels, built under Hadrian (r. 117-138).

building:932787931.desc: Built in the late 2nd century BC and enlarged under Rome to seat about 3,500, Kourion's theater was converted into a beast-fighting arena in the early 3rd century AD; excavated from 1933 by the University of Pennsylvania.

building:958299306.desc: A rock-cut theater built into the hillside at Stymphalos, a planned Classical Arcadian city excavated since 1982 and the legendary site of Herakles' sixth labor, the Stymphalian birds.

building:982593692.desc: Civic sanctuary of the Attic deme Ikarion, birthplace of tragedian Thespis and host of the winter Rural Dionysia; its theater, choregic monument, and temple site were excavated by the American School from 1888-89.

building:983828927.desc: Hellenistic theater at Alinda in Caria, built early 2nd century BC into the acropolis hillside with 35 rows seating roughly 5,000 spectators; renovated under Augustus.

place:dare-20255.desc: The Théâtre romain des Câteliers near Les Andelys (Noyers-sur-Andelys, Eure), discovered by Léon Coutil in 1927; at 120m diameter it ranks among the ten largest theaters of Roman Gaul, built 1st-2nd century AD.

place:pl-177540.desc: Ancient name of the Gallo-Roman town at Entrains-sur-Nohain, attested by a marble itinerary plaque found at Autun in 1840; aerial survey in 1974 revealed a theater about 115m across, among Gaul's largest.

vici:vici-10015.desc: Roman theater on a Treverian sanctuary plateau above the Saar near Kastel-Staadt, excavated 2006-2008; had 36 rows of seating for about 3,500 spectators, rebuilt in stone around 300 AD after an earlier timber phase.

vici:vici-10173.desc: Roman circus of Vienne, built at the end of the 2nd century AD for chariot racing along a roughly 460m track; its spina was marked by 'La Pyramide,' a 23m truncated obelisk on an arched base still standing today.

vici:vici-10202.desc: Roman theater of Aventicum (Avenches), built early in the 2nd century AD with capacity for roughly 11,000 spectators across about 40 rows; converted into a fortified refuge with a ditch in the later 3rd century.

vici:vici-10378.desc: Wooden theater of Nida, Roman civitas capital at Frankfurt-Heddernheim, its seating raised on an earthen bank; excavated in 1928 but now completely destroyed by later urban development.

vici:vici-10448.desc: Theater of Lopodunum, capital of the Civitas Ulpia Sueborum Nicrensium after 98 AD; inscribed stone seats naming individual donors and owners survive, now held in museums including the Badisches Landesmuseum Karlsruhe.

vici:vici-10517.desc: Amphitheater of Marcianopolis (Devnya, Bulgaria), excavated 1958-61 by Goranka Toncheva; measured about 65 by 58m with 12 rows of seating in 14 sections and two arched gates, including the porta libitinensis.

vici:vici-10518.desc: Amphitheater of Gorsium (Tác), the Pannonian provincial town that served as center of the imperial cult and, after rebuilding as Herculia, provincial capital; accommodated up to about 2,500 spectators.

vici:vici-10525.desc: Amphitheatre of Brigetio, a Danube-frontier legionary and civilian settlement (Komárom-Szőny, Hungary) that became a municipium around 205 AD; a 2009 epigraphic study argues the town had two separate amphitheatres, military and civic.

vici:vici-10604.desc: Roman theatre built under Augustus c. 40-30 BC, one of the earliest stone theatres in Gaul, dedicated to Apollo; seated roughly 8,000 and yielded the Venus of Arles, found in pieces in 1651.

vici:vici-10608.desc: Roman circus of Arles, built in 149 AD on the Rhône bank on thousands of wooden piles; 450m long with tiered seating for up to 20,000, used for chariot races. Its spina obelisk now stands in Arles' Place de la République.

vici:vici-10638.desc: Theatre of Gisacum, the vast Gallo-Roman sanctuary town near Évreux; among the largest in Roman Gaul at c. 106m diameter, holding several thousand spectators, its entrance aligned on the adjoining temple's axis.

vici:vici-10693.desc: Gallo-Roman arena-theatre at Thénac ('Les Arènes'), near Saintes; likely built under Nero and abandoned in the 3rd century, cavea roughly 84-90m across, part of a rural sanctuary with baths and a sacred spring.

vici:vici-10762.desc: Theatre of Lugdunum Convenarum (Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges), the Roman town traditionally linked to Pompey's 72 BC foundation; its cavea was remodelled around the mid-1st century AD with a large porticus post scaenam.

vici:vici-10866.desc: Hippodrome (circus) of Antinoöpolis, the city Hadrian founded in 130 AD to honour his drowned favourite Antinous; roughly 307m by 77m, it hosted the Antinoeia games of chariot racing and athletic contests.

vici:vici-10904.desc: The so-called 'Tomba di Agrippina' at Bauli (Bacoli), long linked in legend to Nero's mother but actually a private theatre-nymphaeum (teatro-ninfeo): an Augustan-era odeion within a seaside villa, later converted into a fountain hall.

vici:vici-10906.desc: Theatre remains on the citadel mound of ancient Beroea (Seleucid refoundation of an older Aramaean settlement), later renamed Aleppo; Roman-period Beroea had a colonnaded east-west avenue and a monumental triple arch, with the citadel serving as its acropolis.

vici:vici-10918.desc: Theatre-temple complex of the Samnite Pentri federal sanctuary at Pietrabbondante (identified in older scholarship with the town Bovianum Vetus), built in the late 2nd century BC with its cavea cut into the hillside below the larger Temple B.

vici:vici-10939.desc: Hierapolis Bambyce (modern Manbij, Syria), hellenized from the Aramaic Manbug and known to Greeks as the 'City of the Sanctuary' for its shrine of the Syrian goddess Atargatis, famously described in Lucian's De Dea Syria; distinct from the Phrygian Hierapolis.

vici:vici-10943.desc: Hellenistic theatre of the Carian port city Kaunos near modern Dalyan, seating roughly 5,000 across nine wedges of 33 rows with eight stairways, overlooking the city's silted harbor.

vici:vici-10959.desc: Lower theatre of Cnidus (Knidos) in Caria, built into the hillside beside a temple of Dionysus and holding an estimated 5,000-6,400 spectators; first surveyed by the Society of Dilettanti's 1812 mission and excavated by Charles Newton in 1857-58.

vici:vici-11050.desc: Roman-era (2nd-century AD) theatre of Gythion, Sparta's ancient port on the Laconian Gulf; excavated from 1891 by Andreas Skias, its marble cavea held twelve rows in five wedges with a front row of backed seats reserved for officials.

vici:vici-11094.desc: Theatre of Perinthus, the Samian colony on the Propontis renamed Heraclea under Diocletian; excavations at modern Marmara Ereğlisi have uncovered the theatre together with public baths and the city's fortification walls.

vici:vici-11100.desc: Theatre of Hierapolis (Phrygia), first built in the 2nd century BC, wrecked by the earthquake of 60 AD, then rebuilt under the Flavians in works lasting until c. 206 AD; among Anatolia's largest theatres, with an estimated capacity of 10,000-12,500.

vici:vici-11143.desc: A Hellenistic theatre cut into the hillside at Limyra in Lycia, built in the 2nd-1st century BC with a horseshoe cavea holding about 8,000; badly damaged in the 141 AD earthquake and rebuilt with funds from the Lycian benefactor Opramoas of Rhodiapolis.

vici:vici-11149.desc: The Ancient Theatre of Ohrid, built in Hellenistic style at the end of the 3rd or start of the 2nd century BC, the only theatre of its kind in North Macedonia; Romans later used it for gladiatorial games and it was a site of Christian executions under Diocletian.

vici:vici-11218.desc: Built at the end of the 1st century BC under Augustus near the forum and port of Tarraco, seating about 6,000 spectators over three tiers; part of the UNESCO-listed Archaeological Ensemble of Tárraco.

vici:vici-11241.desc: Spain's smallest surviving Roman theatre, carved directly into the bedrock at Pollentia (Alcúdia, Mallorca) in the 1st century AD to seat 1,500-2,000; excavated from 1952 and later reused as a late-Roman burial ground.

vici:vici-11249.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Mytilene on Lesbos (3rd-1st century BC), its cavea over 100m across; Pompey had its design copied to build Rome's first permanent stone theatre, dedicated in 55 BC.

vici:vici-11259.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman theatre at Neapolis (Samaria, modern Nablus), one of the largest in Roman Syria-Palestine with seating for several thousand; discovered by chance in 1978 during water-canal excavations, later converted into a water basin.

vici:vici-11262.desc: Built under Augustus in the early 1st century AD as part of Nicopolis's Actian Games complex commemorating his victory at Actium, with a 92m-diameter cavea seating roughly 5,000; in continuous use into the 3rd century AD.

vici:vici-11319.desc: Small basalt-built theatre (capacity around 2,000) at Philippopolis, the Hauran village emperor Philip the Arab (r. 244-249) rebuilt as a colonia; construction halted, unfinished, at his death.

vici:vici-11324.desc: Hellenistic theatre (15 tiers, capacity ~3,000) of ancient Phthiotic Thebes, identified and partly excavated in 1992–93; in the Roman period it was remodeled into an arena for wild-beast fights.

vici:vici-11325.desc: Well-preserved Hellenistic theatre of Pinara in Lycia, built into a hillside in the late 2nd century BC with a capacity of about 3,200; nearly all its stone seating survives intact.

vici:vici-11345.desc: Theatre of ancient Samos near Pythagoreion, on the slope below the sanctuary of Panagia Spiliani; construction is dated to the 4th century BC with Roman-era rebuilding, though little masonry survives today.

vici:vici-11353.desc: Mountain theatre of Selge in Pisidia, built in the 2nd century AD with a 104 m cavea seating roughly 9,000–10,000; later reduced and converted into an arena in the 3rd century.

vici:vici-11403.desc: Rural Gallo-Roman theatre at Les Bardiaux (Arleuf, Morvan), built around the late 2nd century AD with six concentric tiers seating 600–700; discovered in 1971, it likely also hosted public assemblies.

vici:vici-11406.desc: Large Gallo-Roman theatre (110 m diameter, seating up to 8,000–10,000) at the sanctuary of Les Vaux-de-la-Celle, Genainville; sometimes, inconclusively, identified with the Peutinger Table's station of Petromantalum/Petrum Viacum.

vici:vici-11409.desc: Roman theatre of Teanum Sidicinum (Teano), among Italy's largest with an 85 m cavea seating 6,000–7,000; built from the late 2nd century BC, systematically excavated by Werner Johannowsky, 1961–1976.

vici:vici-11413.desc: Despite being labeled 'Thapsus,' this theatre stands at Rusicade (modern Skikda), the largest Roman theatre in Algeria, built under Hadrian; long quarried for stone, only fragments survive today.

vici:vici-11414.desc: Ancient Greek theatre of Thasos, cut into the acropolis hillside; first built early 3rd century BC with a marble stage front dedicated to Dionysus by Lysistratos, later used from the 1st century AD for beast hunts and gladiatorial combat.

vici:vici-11441.desc: Best-preserved structure at Tusculum: a Roman theatre with a 45m cavea and 17.5m orchestra, built early 1st century AD and excavated by Luigi Canina in 1839-40 for Lucien Bonaparte's campaign.

vici:vici-11444.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman hippodrome at Tyre, 480m by 90m and seating 20,000, among the largest and best-preserved in the Roman world; hosted the Heraclia games for Melqart-Heracles every four years.

vici:vici-11449.desc: Roman odeon on the Fourvière hill in Lyon (Lugdunum), built early-mid 2nd century AD with a 73m diameter seating 3,000; one of only two paired odeon-theatre complexes in Roman Gaul, alongside Vienne.

vici:vici-11462.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre at Vieux-Poitiers (ancient Vetus Pictavis, Naintré), 116m in diameter and seating an estimated 10,000 - larger than the theatre of Orange - hosting drama and gladiatorial combat for two centuries.

vici:vici-11463.desc: Theatre of the Roman vicus Augusti (modern Sidi el-Hani, Tunisia), with a 58m cavea and 22m orchestra; its ruins, with radial vaulted substructures, were first studied by Saladin and Cagnat in 1882.

vici:vici-11524.desc: Stone Roman theatre built AD 161 on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis by Herodes Atticus in memory of his wife Aspasia Annia Regilla; seated about 5,000 and still hosts the Athens Festival today.

vici:vici-11525.desc: Athens's original odeon, built 435 BC by Pericles for the musical contests of the Panathenaea, its square timber roof said to imitate Xerxes's tent; burned during Sulla's siege in 86 BC and later rebuilt.

vici:vici-11528.desc: The smaller of Gadara's two Roman theatres, built of basalt on the acropolis's western slope in the Severan period (193-235 AD) with seating for about 3,000; 1960s excavations uncovered a marble statue of Tyche, now in the Umm Qais museum.

vici:vici-11532.desc: Begun under Septimius Severus around AD 203 and enlarged by Constantine I after 330 as Constantinople's chariot-racing arena and ceremonial center; site of the AD 532 Nika riots, its spina still marked by an Egyptian obelisk and the Serpent Column.

vici:vici-11548.desc: Built as a bouleuterion around the 1st century BC and rebuilt as an odeon around AD 160; Strabo calls it a gerontikon, the meeting hall of the city elders, with 12 rows of seats holding roughly 600-700 people.

vici:vici-11555.desc: Roman theatre built in the city founded under Hadrian (who visited the region in AD 125); its cavea, 58 m across with 24 limestone rows, seated about 3,500-4,000. Rediscovered after a 1970s landslide, confirmed as Hadrianopolis by 2002 excavations.

vici:vici-11564.desc: Late Hellenistic or early imperial theatre of Magnesia on the Maeander, left unfinished and only discovered by excavation in 1984; the cavea survives but no trace remains of its probably wooden stage building.

vici:vici-11565.desc: Built around 370 BC by the architect Polycleitus the Younger for the newly founded federal capital of the Arcadian League; Pausanias called it the largest theatre in Greece, holding up to about 20,000 and doubling as the League's assembly hall.

vici:vici-11571.desc: Roman odeon of Patras built in the first half of the 2nd century AD, praised by Pausanias as second only to the Odeon of Herodes Atticus; destroyed by fire in the late 3rd century, rediscovered in 1889, and restored in 1959-61.

vici:vici-11591.desc: Theatre of Uthina (Oudhna), near the Numidian-Roman colony's forum and capitol; unlike the city's well-preserved 16,000-seat amphitheatre, it survives only as a shallow depression in the hillside.

vici:vici-11593.desc: Hellenistic theatre on Pergamon's upper acropolis, built c. 225-200 BC and enlarged under Eumenes II; the steepest theatre of the ancient world, with 78 rows seating about 10,000, fronted by a 247m colonnaded terrace and the Temple of Dionysus.

vici:vici-11598.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre at Les Bouchauds (ancient Germanicomagus) in Charente, cut into a hillside beside a sanctuary; at 105.6m diameter the largest theatre in Aquitaine, seating 5,000-6,000, discovered in 1865 and protected as a historic monument since 1881.

vici:vici-11671.desc: Circus built by Tiberius in AD 14 at Bovillae, ancestral town of the Julian family, in honor of the deified Augustus; measured c. 337 x 69m, with yearly imperial-cult games instituted there from AD 16.

vici:vici-11713.desc: Roman amphitheatre of Tarentum (Taranto), southwest of the Chiesa del Carmine, possibly built over one of the city's two unexcavated Greek theatres; no remains are visible today.

vici:vici-11759.desc: Small Roman theatre within Pergamon's Asclepieion healing sanctuary, seating about 3,500; built into the sanctuary's northwest corner, with performances forming part of the complex's ritual therapy during its Hadrianic-era remodeling.

vici:vici-11768.desc: Stadium of Ephesus, first laid out by Lysimachus in the 3rd century BC and rebuilt for chariot races, athletics, and beast/gladiator fights under Nero; U-shaped, 229 x 28m, its southern seating cut into the slope of Mount Pion.

vici:vici-11769.desc: Small covered theater built c. 150 AD by the wealthy Ephesian Publius Vedius Antoninus and his wife Flavia Papiana; seated roughly 1,400–1,500 and served both as a concert hall (odeon) and as the city's council chamber (bouleuterion).

vici:vici-11776.desc: Roman stadium of Philippopolis, built in the early 2nd century AD under Hadrian, about 240 m long and seating up to 30,000; its curved northern end (sphendone) survives exposed beneath Plovdiv's main pedestrian street.

vici:vici-11840.desc: Stadium of Aphrodisias, built in the later 1st century AD, c. 270 x 60 m with 30 tiers of seating for up to 30,000; considered the best-preserved stadium of the ancient world and used for athletic and artistic contests.

vici:vici-11843.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Assos, built around the late 4th–3rd century BC on a slope overlooking the Aegean with views to Lesbos; seated roughly 5,000 and gained a two-storey stage building in the Roman period.

vici:vici-11846.desc: Greco-Roman theatre of Myra, badly damaged in the 141 AD earthquake and rebuilt with funds from the Lycian benefactor Opramoas of Rhodiapolis; seated up to about 12,000, making it the largest theatre in Lycia.

vici:vici-11879.desc: Theatre at the Gallo-Roman sanctuary complex of Cherré (Aubigné-Racan, Sarthe), a 1st–3rd century AD rural cult and market center excavated since 1977 that also included two temples, baths, an aqueduct, and a forum.

vici:vici-11915.desc: Roman theatre built by Herod the Great (37–4 BC) at Caesarea Maritima, among the earliest Roman-style theatres in the region; seated roughly 3,500–4,000 and, after restoration, is still used for performances today.

vici:vici-11916.desc: Monumental hippodrome/stadium built by Herod the Great, dedicated with the city in 10/9 BC and roughly 315 m long; hosted the quadrennial Actian Games (chariot racing, athletics, gladiatorial combat) and was later remodeled as an amphitheater.

vici:vici-11918.desc: Theatre serving Anderitum, Gallo-Roman capital of the Gabali civitas at Javols; built between the 1st and 3rd centuries AD, possibly a mixed theatre-amphitheatre, with only the perimeter wall and foundations surviving.

vici:vici-11924.desc: The rock-hewn theatre (or odeion) of Roman Canatha (Qanawat) in the Hauran, cut into the wadi slope with nine tiers of basalt seating around a 19m orchestra; likely 3rd-century, possibly cult-related.

vici:vici-11932.desc: Ancient Greek theatre of Mantinea in Arcadia, built into the city's agora complex after its 371 BC reconstruction following destruction by Sparta; unusually raised entirely on an artificial mound rather than a hillside.

vici:vici-11933.desc: Small theatre cut into bedrock at Melli (Kocaaliler) in Pisidia; a 2000 Pisidia Survey Project excavation suggested a Severan-era (early 3rd-century AD) construction date, though the city's ancient name remains unknown.

vici:vici-11972.desc: A rare rural Romano-British theatre-sanctuary near Faversham, Kent, excavated 2007-2013; a circular 'cockpit-style' auditorium set within a religious complex with two temple enclosures and a sacred spring, apparently used for ceremonial gatherings.

vici:vici-11974.desc: Large elliptical Gallo-Roman amphitheatre of Rotomagus (Rouen), built late in the 2nd century AD and among the largest in Gaul at roughly 130 by 120 metres; abandoned by the 4th century and later buried under Philip Augustus's castle.

vici:vici-11986.desc: The Small Roman Theatre of Pula, built in the 1st century AD on the hillside below the citadel; seated an estimated 4,000-5,000 for drama and recitations, with 2011-2022 excavations restoring the site.

vici:vici-11991.desc: The only known Roman-era theatre in Thrace, built in the 2nd century AD at Bizye (Vize); discovered in 1998, its marble cavea seated some 4,000, with stage reliefs now held in Kırklareli Museum.

vici:vici-12001.desc: Built in AD 79 and dedicated to Vespasian and Titus, funded privately by the citizen Nikostratos; at roughly 285 x 70 m this was one of Roman Anatolia's largest stadiums, seating up to 25,000 for games.

vici:vici-12005.desc: A 1st-century AD Roman theatre built into a hillside at Hierapolis Kastabala in Cilicia, seating roughly 5,000 across ima and summa cavea; excavations have recovered 36 theatrical stage masks, including five found in 2025.

vici:vici-12007.desc: A small, well-preserved covered odeon/bouleuterion at Anemurium in Rough Cilicia, excavated with the rest of the city (UBC excavations, 1965-1993), seating roughly 900-1,100 for council meetings or performances.

vici:vici-12008.desc: Anemurium's main Roman theatre, a semicircular structure about 60 m in diameter, built in the 3rd century AD at the height of the city's prosperity.

vici:vici-12019.desc: A Roman-period stadium at the foot of the Sardis acropolis, south of the gymnasium, with a U-shaped, double-sided cavea built against vaulted substructures on the slope.

vici:vici-12020.desc: Theater built at Sardis during the Hellenistic period as part of the city's conversion into a Greek-style polis; it stood alongside a stadium among Sardis's Roman-era civic monuments.

vici:vici-12057.desc: Roman theater at Shuni (ancient Kfar Shumi) built with an adjoining semicircular pool used for the Graeco-Syrian Maiumas water festival honoring Dionysus and Aphrodite; excavated from 1986 by E. Shenhav for the JNF.

vici:vici-12877.desc: The only known Roman theater in Noricum, built in the 2nd century AD with a semicircular cavea of 35 m radius and a 32.5 m stage, near Virunum's amphitheater; excavated 1890s–1931 and again since the 1990s.

vici:vici-12885.desc: Small roofed odeon at Nicopolis ad Istrum (founded by Trajan c. 102–106) seating up to about 350; its stone cavea was supported on arches sheltering eleven shops, separated from the semicircular orchestra by iron-braced stone blocks.

vici:vici-13588.desc: Roman amphitheater at Tomis, arena c. 60x40 m within a complex up to 100x80 m and 20–25 m tall, discovered in 1980; a 2nd–3rd century AD section found in 1988–89 was preserved beneath the Ibis hotel.

vici:vici-13914.desc: Roman circus built around 293 AD under Maximian near his Aquileia residence for chariot racing; still in use in 425, when the usurper John (Johannes) was executed there on Valentinian III's orders.

vici:vici-14461.desc: A wall with buttresses at Arae Flaviae (Rottweil), near a sanctuary and blocks resembling seating stones, has been tentatively identified as a theater on the strength of geomagnetic surveys showing a monumental complex, though the evidence remains inconclusive.

vici:vici-14560.desc: Gallo-Roman theater built in the 2nd century AD within a rural pilgrimage complex with a water sanctuary; held about 3,000 spectators, was abandoned by the late 3rd century, excavated from the 1950s, and protected as a historic monument since 1983.

vici:vici-14567.desc: 1st-century Gallo-Roman theatre (57m diameter) at Saint-Germain-d'Esteuil in the Médoc; the only known Gallo-Roman theatre in the Aquitaine basin south of the Garonne, abandoned by the mid-3rd century and identified during 1966 excavations.

vici:vici-14576.desc: Theatre of Divona Cadurcorum (Roman Cahors), built c. 41-54 AD under Claudius; once the best-preserved Roman monument in Cahors until its owner demolished the remaining structure in 1860-1863.

vici:vici-14582.desc: Small Gallo-Roman theatre near Saint-Goussaud (Creuse), reputedly the smallest in France; a semicircular seating structure about 9m wide, excavated in 1901, 1965, and 1980, near a Roman road linking Augustoritum (Limoges) and Ahun.

vici:vici-14785.desc: Odeon (concert hall) built at Ilion (Troy) under Augustus on the site of the old bouleuterion, extensively renovated by Hadrian after his AD 124 visit; a marble cuirassed statue of Hadrian was found here in 1993.

vici:vici-14869.desc: Roman theatre within the Asclepieion healing sanctuary of Pergamon, built under Hadrian in the 2nd century AD to host therapeutic musical and dramatic performances for patients; seated roughly 3,500 in a hillside cavea with a marble-paved orchestra.

vici:vici-14871.desc: Best-preserved building of ancient Teos in Ionia; a late 3rd-century BC council hall (bouleuterion) seating about 800, later adapted in the Roman period with an added stage building for theatrical use.

vici:vici-15049.desc: Sunken garden-stadium (160 x 48m) built by Domitian (81-92 AD) on the east side of his Domus Augustana palace on the Palatine; despite its racetrack shape it was too small for chariots and served as an imperial pleasure garden.

vici:vici-15097.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman odeon at Nea Paphos, Cyprus, built of dressed limestone with twelve rows of seating for roughly 1,200; restored by the Cyprus Department of Antiquities and still used for concerts and drama festivals.

vici:vici-15709.desc: Roman-era stadium (186 m long) at Magnesia on the Maeander seating up to 30,000, built in the 1st century AD for the Artemis Leucophryene games; hosted athletics, gladiatorial fights and chariot races; excavated by Carl Humann in 1891-93, resumed 1984.

vici:vici-15711.desc: Ancient theatre at Phocaea (Foça), believed to be the oldest Ionian theatre yet discovered; partially excavated on the acropolis hill, first investigated by Félix Sartiaux in 1913-1920 and systematically by Ekrem Akurgal from 1951.

vici:vici-16324.desc: Roman circus of Hadrumetum (Sousse), attested by circus-themed mosaics of the four racing factions and by curse tablets targeting charioteers recorded in Audollent's Defixionum tabellae; its physical remains have not been precisely excavated in situ.

vici:vici-16584.desc: Greek-style theatre of Massalia (Marseille), built in the Augustan period, about 115 m wide with a 54 m stage, seating roughly 6,000; located near the Vieux-Port, first found in 1946-48 excavations and further excavated 1961-64.

vici:vici-16587.desc: Roman theatre of Baeterrae (Béziers), unexpectedly discovered in 2014 during a preventive excavation near Place des Chaudronniers; about a third of the building survives, with a restored diameter of 80-85 m, sited between the forum and amphitheatre.

vici:vici-16613.desc: Roman theatre of Aquae Sextiae (Aix-en-Provence), about 100 m in diameter and built in the Julio-Claudian period on a terrace of the western quarter; long only suspected, it was confirmed by excavations at Notre-Dame-de-la-Seds in 2004.

vici:vici-16628.desc: A Roman theatre certainly existed at Nemausus (Nîmes), a major colonial city, but unlike its well-preserved amphitheatre its precise location has never been archaeologically identified, with the site only loosely placed near the cathedral.

vici:vici-16629.desc: Roman theatre of Apta Julia (Apt), built in the early 1st century AD, roughly 80x60 m and holding 5,000-6,000 spectators; substantial remains were found beneath Place Carnot in 1869-70 and confirmed by soundings in 1966-67.

vici:vici-16644.desc: Roman theatre in Aginnum (Agen), built in the 1st century AD with a diameter of about 86m; repaired several times before abandonment in the early 2nd century, its foundations remain buried near Place Armand-Fallières.

vici:vici-16656.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre near Rodez, with an estimated 2,000-seat cavea, built within a rural sanctuary complex in the 1st-2nd century AD; discovered in 1865 by Abbé Cérès, with a coin of Marcus Aurelius (145 AD) aiding its dating.

vici:vici-16718.desc: 1st-century Gallo-Roman theatre near Saint-Germain-d'Esteuil in the Médoc, 57m in diameter with an estimated 2,200 seats, built in opus mixtum masonry; the only known Gallo-Roman theatre south of the Garonne, identified in 1966.

vici:vici-16736.desc: Small hemicycle of tiered seating (a fifth of a circle) built in early-1st-century Vienne, interpreted as a theatre for the mystery rites of Cybele and Attis or as a civic auditorium; now part of the Jardin archéologique de Cybèle.

vici:vici-16740.desc: Roman theatre on La Garde hill within the Gallo-Roman port city at Barzan (site du Fâ); hemicycle radius of at least 50m, among the largest in Gaul, rivaling Vienne, Orange and Autun; built late 1st century AD.

vici:vici-16744.desc: Mixed Gallo-Roman theatre at Aquae Segetae (Moingt), built mid-1st century, enlarged in the 2nd, seating up to ~8,000 in a 42m-radius semicircle; its ruined wall, known locally as the Mur des Sarrasins, was classified a historic monument in 1981.

vici:vici-16771.desc: Roman circus for chariot racing at Lugdunum (Lyon), attested by inscriptions but never securely located archaeologically; probable remains found near rue Pauline-Marie Jaricot may correspond to it.

vici:vici-16773.desc: Roman theatre on the Puy de Montaudou above Ceyrat, near Augustonemetum (Clermont-Ferrand); built late 1st century at 51m diameter, enlarged to 81m in the 2nd; identified as a theatre, not a Mercury sanctuary, in 2005 by Christian Le Barrier.

vici:vici-16846.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre-amphitheatre of Argentomagus, rebuilt mid-2nd century CE to an 85m diameter, succeeding a smaller theatre built under Tiberius; abandoned by the early 5th century.

vici:vici-16866.desc: Theatre identified in 1874 as part of a Gallo-Roman religious complex with sanctuary and necropolis near Alléans, one of the most important such sites in the Cher; excavated through the 19th century.

vici:vici-16908.desc: Urban theatre of the Gallo-Roman town of Compierre, built in the 3rd century CE with a 72m cavea seating about 5,000; the settlement was abandoned by the late 4th century.

vici:vici-16919.desc: 1st-century Gallo-Roman theatre near the Vieille-Cour sanctuary to Mars Mullo, excavated from 1886 by Léon Maître; cavea over 61m across, its wooden seating and stage now vanished.

vici:vici-16929.desc: Earthwork amphitheatre within the legionary fortress at Mirebeau, built c. 70 CE for Legio VIII Augusta's roughly 5,000 troops under Vespasian; its embankments are still visible today.

vici:vici-16933.desc: A theatre serving the civilian canabae outside the Mirebeau legionary fortress of Legio VIII Augusta (70–86 CE) was later restored by a Roman citizen's daughter after falling into ruin.

vici:vici-16947.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre of the Venetic settlement near Vannes, its cavea extending past a semicircle beyond the standard hemicycle; excavated by Christophe-Paul de Robien (18th c.) and Closmadeuc, partly overtaken by the village cemetery.

vici:vici-16991.desc: Gallo-Roman amphitheatre at La Mardelle, 71 by 56m with room for about 8,000 spectators, found around 1850; its dressed stones were later reused to build the 1862 cemetery wall.

vici:vici-17038.desc: The 80m-diameter theatre at Vorganium, the only known theatre in Osismii territory, stood outside the settlement, identified via a nearby Kerscao milestone naming the site; excavated in the 1960s, never fully cleared, and abandoned after 3rd-century raids.

vici:vici-17042.desc: Nasium, first capital of the Leuci civitas, held a theatre on the Mazeroie plateau, identified in 1833 by antiquarian Claude-François Denis at a spot locals called the 'Fosse aux Lions', proving it a theatre rather than a circus.

vici:vici-17065.desc: The Gallo-Roman theatre of Arnières-sur-Iton was exposed during Paris-Caen railway construction and partly excavated by Théodore Bonnin in 1853; it stood by a roadside settlement on the Évreux-Condé-sur-Iton route, occupied from the 1st to the 4th century.

vici:vici-17072.desc: Built around 100 CE in Divodurum Mediomatricorum's Sablon district, this amphitheatre's 158m major axis made it the third-largest in the Roman world after the Colosseum and Capua; used for games until the 3rd century, rediscovered during 1902 railway excavations.

vici:vici-17090.desc: Built in the later 1st century in Augusta Suessionum, capital of the Suessiones, its 144m cavea rivaled Autun's as one of Gaul's largest theatres, said to seat 20,000; dismantled for stone from the 3rd century onward.

vici:vici-17091.desc: A modest 'rustic' Gallo-Roman theatre seating up to 2,000 stood in Lyons-la-Forêt's Bout-de-Bas district on the Rouen-Beauvais road where it crossed the Lieure; 3rd-century in date, excavated in the 20th century by Marc-Adrien Dollfus.

vici:vici-17095.desc: Catalogued as a possible theatre at Rotomagus (Roman Rouen), but no archaeological remains have ever been confirmed here; the city's only firmly attested Roman spectacle building is its separate amphitheatre.

vici:vici-17097.desc: Gallo-Roman arena-theatre at Alauna (Valognes), built in the later 1st century AD, over 72 m across with capacity of roughly 3,700-4,000; excavated from 1695, largely quarried away in the 1820s-30s.

vici:vici-17126.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre built in a single campaign around the mid-2nd century AD and abandoned within about 50 years; 75 m cavea diameter; rediscovered in 1977 under a mound long taken for a medieval earthwork.

vici:vici-17193.desc: One of the spectacle buildings of the major Ribemont-sur-Ancre sanctuary, a Celtic ritual site (mass ossuary deposits) succeeded by a Gallo-Roman complex of temple, theatre and baths; identified from 1962 aerial survey.

vici:vici-17194.desc: Theatre of the Ribemont-sur-Ancre sanctuary, seating roughly 3,000; confirmed as a theatre in 1971 excavations, part of a temple-theatre-baths complex built over an earlier Celtic sanctuary marked by mass human bone deposits.

vici:vici-17229.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman stadium at Perge, among the largest and best-preserved in Asia Minor at 234 m long, seating around 12,000; its vaulted substructure held shops, per surviving inscriptions.

vici:vici-17230.desc: U-shaped 2nd-century AD stadium at Aspendos, about 220 x 30 m with an estimated 8,000 seats; far less well preserved than the city's famous Roman theatre nearby.

vici:vici-17233.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman theatre at Phaselis in Lycia, built over an earlier Hellenistic theatre; cavea about 46-50 m across in 5 wedges, seating roughly 1,500-2,000.

vici:vici-17234.desc: A small theatre with an elaborate entrance, likely Hellenistic in origin but rebuilt under Rome; Olympos held three votes in the Lycian League and was visited by Hadrian in 129 CE.

vici:vici-17236.desc: A rock-cut theatre with seven rows carved into the bedrock of Simena's acropolis, probably Hellenistic and among the smallest and oldest theatres in Lycia; later enclosed within a medieval castle.

vici:vici-17238.desc: Roman theatre of Elaiussa Sebaste in Cilicia, a modest structure of about 23 rows of seats built in the 2nd century AD; the city has been excavated since 1995 by Rome's Sapienza University.

vici:vici-17271.desc: Rock-cut theatre below the Lindos acropolis, dated to the 4th century BC with seating for roughly 1,800-2,000; hosted the Sminthia festival of Dionysos with drama, music and athletic contests.

vici:vici-17273.desc: Hellenistic stadium on the Acropolis of Rhodes, 210 m long and built in the 3rd century BC; hosted the athletic events of the Alieia games honoring Helios, later restored under Italian rule after 1912.

vici:vici-17274.desc: Marble odeon on the Acropolis of Rhodes, built in the 3rd-2nd century BC as part of the gymnasium complex; seated about 800 for musical performances and rhetoric lessons, reconstructed under Italian rule.

vici:vici-17313.desc: Roman theatre of Osca, first identified in 2006 beneath Huesca's old town near Calle Canellas; built in the first half of the 1st century AD, possibly larger than Zaragoza's Augustan-era theatre.

vici:vici-17315.desc: One of three known spectacle buildings (theatre, amphitheatre, circus) at Valeria, a city founded by proconsul Gaius Valerius Flaccus c. 93-82 BC; still largely unexcavated, with only a small fraction of the hillside site uncovered.

vici:vici-17316.desc: A possible amphitheatre at the Celtiberian-Roman town of Nertobriga Concordia Iulia, proposed from an oval crop-mark visible in early-2000s SIGPAC aerial orthophotos; never excavated, and its identification as an amphitheatre remains debated among researchers.

vici:vici-17319.desc: No archaeological remains confirm an amphitheatre at Calagurris; only the Roman circus (traceable in the modern street plan, c. 360 x 80 m) is archaeologically attested, though scholars presume a theatre and amphitheatre existed given the city's civic status.

vici:vici-17321.desc: Built in the second half of the 1st century AD under the Julio-Claudians outside Toletum's walls in the Vega Baja, one of Hispania's largest circuses (up to 427 m long), seating 14,000-30,000 for chariot races until the 5th century.

vici:vici-17322.desc: Circus of Roman Saguntum, built for chariot racing outside the walls near the Palancia (ancient Udiva) river; roughly 354 x 73 m with a 190 m spina, seating an estimated 15,000-20,000 spectators.

vici:vici-17323.desc: Theatre of Colonia Iulia Gemella Acci, built around 25 AD on the hillside above the Roman colony founded for veterans after the Battle of Munda; discovered by chance in 2007 during construction work, covering some 6,000 sq m.

vici:vici-17325.desc: Small Roman theatre of Baetulo, built in the early 2nd century AD with an estimated original capacity of about 878 spectators; the second known Roman theatre in Catalonia after Tarragona's, still under excavation.

vici:vici-17344.desc: Hippodrome of Roman Bostra, an elongated arena roughly 420 x 97 m built after Arabia became a Roman province in 106 AD, possibly linked to athletic games founded under Philip the Arab (244-249); one of five known Roman hippodromes in the Levant.

vici:vici-17346.desc: One of the largest theatres of the Roman world, built in the 2nd century AD with a cavea 139 m in diameter seating over 20,000; only the Theatre of Pompey in Rome was larger. Its design gave exceptional acoustics, audible from any seat.

vici:vici-17347.desc: Roman theatre south of the agora at Dionysias (Soada, modern Suweida), part of an Odeion-Bouleuterion-theatre-colonnade complex built around 100 CE as the region became the province of Arabia.

vici:vici-17348.desc: The theatre of Acholla (Henchir Botria), a Punic-founded city that submitted to Caesar in 46 BC; monumental elite-funded building in the 2nd-3rd centuries AD added this theatre alongside an amphitheatre and bath complexes.

vici:vici-17349.desc: The Roman circus of Thysdrus (El Jem), a chariot-racing track almost as large as Rome's Circus Maximus with capacity for up to 30,000 spectators, built as the city rivaled Hadrumetum as North Africa's second city.

vici:vici-17351.desc: The Roman circus of Caesarea in Mauretania (Cherchell), the capital built up under Juba II and Cleopatra Selene; unlike the theatre and amphitheatre, which align with the city's cardo maximus, the circus follows a different orientation tied to the surrounding land division.

vici:vici-17353.desc: Theatre of the Roman colony Scupi (Colonia Scupi Aelia), built in the 2nd century AD and identified by seat inscriptions bearing the colony's name; discovered by archaeologist Nikola Vulić and since fully excavated, yielding over 8,500 artifacts, though the structure itself survives only in decayed form.

vici:vici-17358.desc: Not a theatre but the bouleuterion (council house) of Stratonicea near Eskihisar/Yatağan, a rectangular hall with semicircular seating dated to the late 1st century BC-1st century AD, its walls inscribed with a fragment of Diocletian's Prices Edict and a Greek calendar poem attributed to the local orator Menippus.

vici:vici-17359.desc: The Roman-period theatre of Iasos (Iassos), cut into a hillside on the Carian coast with a rock-and-masonry cavea seating roughly 3,500; its ashlar retaining wall was stripped of marble in 1887 to build the quays of Constantinople's harbour.

vici:vici-17361.desc: The theatre-stadium complex at Aizanoi joins a 20,000-seat theatre directly to a 13,500-seat stadium via a shared stage building, a design unique in the ancient world; built c.160-250 AD, funded by M. Apuleius Eurycles.

vici:vici-17367.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Cedrae (Kedreai) on Sedir Island, built in the 4th century BC on a seaward slope outside the walls; seated roughly 2,500, with cavea, orchestra and skene remains, part still unexcavated.

vici:vici-17370.desc: Theatre of Cyme, chief city of the Aeolian dodecapolis; largely unexcavated but identified and mapped via ground-penetrating radar alongside the city's agora, Isis sanctuary and Roman baths.

vici:vici-17372.desc: Roman theatre of Olba (Diocaesarea), built under the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus (r. AD 161-169), seated about 2,000; its auditorium and stage building survive beside the city's nymphaeum.

vici:vici-17376.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Erythrae, one of the twelve Ionian cities, first built in the earlier 3rd century BC and later remodeled with a double diazoma under Hadrian; excavated by Ekrem Akurgal.

vici:vici-17378.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Metropolis in Ionia, built into a natural hillside depression in the 3rd century BC; a marble seat of honor carved with griffins was found there in 1995 during Sabancı Foundation-sponsored excavations.

vici:vici-17380.desc: Theatre of Sillyon (Syllion), a Pamphylian hill city that alone resisted Alexander the Great in 333 BC; documented by Karol Lanckoroński in the 19th century before a 1969 landslide destroyed it along with the adjoining odeon.

vici:vici-17381.desc: Well-preserved Hellenistic theatre of Alinda in Caria, seating roughly 5,000, probably built in the early 2nd century BC and later expanded by the Romans in the 1st century AD.

vici:vici-17382.desc: Physcus (Physkos), a Carian port under Rhodian control with a harbour sacred to Leto; its Hellenistic walls crown Asartepe hill above Marmaris, besieged during Alexander's Carian campaign of 334 BC.

vici:vici-17384.desc: Marble theater of Hyllarima, built in the 2nd-1st century BC and implying a town of roughly 1,500 people; excavated by a French-Turkish team led by Pierre Debord and Ender Varinlioğlu from 1997, published 2018.

vici:vici-17388.desc: Roman theater of Ancyra (Ankara), dated to the 2nd century AD with about 20-22 rows seating 3,000-5,000; excavated 1982-86 and 2009-10, yielding fragments of a cuirassed statue of Hadrian.

vici:vici-17390.desc: Stadium of Cibyra (Kibyra), about 200m long with 21 rows of seats cut into the hillside and a U-shaped sphendone; used for races and, per its famous friezes, gladiatorial combats.

vici:vici-17392.desc: Odeon-bouleuterion of Cibyra, built mid-2nd century AD as the city council chamber and later given a stage; one of Turkey's best-preserved roofed theaters, its orchestra holds the world's only surviving Medusa mosaic in opus sectile.

vici:vici-17393.desc: Theater of Adada, a Pisidian hill town near Sütçüler allied with Termessos against Selge from the 2nd century BC and prosperous under Trajan, Hadrian and Antoninus Pius; its ruins also include a forum, basilica and acropolis.

vici:vici-17394.desc: Theater of Prusias ad Hypium, locally called "The Forty Stairs"; begun in the Hellenistic period and expanded under Rome to 100m x 74m with 36 rows seating about 10,000, its scaenae added around 190 AD.

vici:vici-17396.desc: Theater of Heraclea ad Latmum (Latmos), a Hellenistic city founded in the early 3rd century BC at the foot of Mount Latmus by Lake Bafa, near the temple of Athena Latmia; its seating remains largely unexcavated.

vici:vici-17400.desc: Roman stadium built in the early 2nd century AD under Hadrian, seating up to 30,000 for athletic games; its northern curved end (sphendone) survives beneath Plovdiv's main pedestrian street, discovered in 1923.

vici:vici-17439.desc: The Stadium of Antoninus Pius, built c. 142 AD at Puteoli to honor Hadrian and host Greek-style games (the Eusebeia); one of the largest Roman masonry stadiums after Domitian's, only unearthed in 2008.

vici:vici-17440.desc: Built by Domitian and inaugurated in 86 AD for Greek-style athletic contests (the Capitoline Games), seating c. 30,000; its elongated plan is preserved by Piazza Navona above, with ruins visible underground.

vici:vici-17441.desc: A rectangular circus/hippodrome garden (95 x 327 m) within the huge Villa dei Sette Bassi on Via Tuscolana, second-largest suburban villa after the Villa of the Quintilii; the medieval name likely derives from Caracalla, not a prefect Septimius Bassus.

vici:vici-17446.desc: Covered theatre (theatrum tectum) at the Roman colony of Luna, built c. 40 AD for readings and music, richly decorated with marble sculpture; excavated from the 1950s-70s and converted to housing, then a Lombard necropolis, by the 6th century.

vici:vici-17449.desc: 2nd-century AD odeon adjoining the Roman theatre of Catania, seating about 1,500 for poetry and music contests and rehearsals; built partly of volcanic stone with a purely decorative lava architrave.

vici:vici-17457.desc: Roman amphitheatre of Sulci (Sant'Antioco), identified in 1983-84 on the slope below the Punic acropolis and necropolis; dated to the 2nd-3rd century AD, likely built largely in wood so little of the superstructure survives.

vici:vici-17462.desc: Hellenistic-Roman theatre of Nuceria Alfaterna, built into the Samnite city walls in the 2nd century BC and rebuilt in brick under Augustus; among Campania's largest at 96m diameter, with a marble-paved orchestra and twin vaulted parodoi.

vici:vici-17466.desc: The only known Roman theatre in Sardinia, built in the early 1st century AD; its two-tiered cavea (37.7m diameter, 11 andesite-block steps) held roughly 1,000-1,200 spectators and is still used for summer performances.

vici:vici-17469.desc: Theatre of Publius Vedius Pollio's seaside villa at Pausilypon (Posillipo), built c. 31 BC; part of a 9-hectare estate with baths and an odeon, its cavea overlooking the Bay of Naples seated about 2,000.

vici:vici-17470.desc: Small odeon adjoining the theatre within Vedius Pollio's Pausilypon villa complex, built c. 31 BC as a roofed hall for music and recitation on the same Posillipo estate later inherited by Augustus.

vici:vici-17472.desc: Remains of a Greek theatre (4th-3rd century BC) at the southern end of ancient Lipara's acropolis, its sea view later blocked by 16th-century fortifications; Lipari is separately famed for its hoard of Hellenistic terracotta theatrical masks.

vici:vici-17473.desc: Greek theatre of Iaitas, built before 300 BC on the model of Athens' Theatre of Dionysus; its 35-tier cavea, excavated by the University of Zurich since 1971, seated an estimated 4,000-5,000 spectators.

vici:vici-17474.desc: Roman theatre of Augusta Taurinorum, built c. 13 BC as one of the new colony's first public buildings under Augustus; its four-sector stone cavea seated about 3,500 and was rediscovered in 1899 during construction of Palazzo Reale's new wing.

vici:vici-17480.desc: Roman theatre remains uncovered on the north flank of Sidon's castle hill in modern excavations; little else about the structure has been published.

vici:vici-17481.desc: Small Roman theatre built around AD 218, excavated by Pierre Montet in the 1920s; only five tiers of seating survive today, and it was later dismantled and re-erected closer to the shore during subsequent excavation.

vici:vici-17486.desc: The largest and oldest of Cyrene's five theatres, cut into the Acropolis slope in the late 6th century BC as a Greek theatre; converted into a Roman amphitheatre for gladiatorial games in the late 2nd century AD.

vici:vici-17489.desc: An odeon within the Asclepieion sanctuary at Balagrae, used for musical performances and sophists' recitations as part of the healing cult of Asclepius; a hoard of Roman coins was discovered there in 1956.

vici:vici-17490.desc: The smallest of Ptolemais's three theatres, built as an odeon for music and oratory; in the 4th-5th century AD its orchestra and stage were lined with waterproof cement to form a pool, possibly for mock sea-battles or acoustics.

vici:vici-17496.desc: Roman theatre of Civitas Camunnorum, built around the mid-1st century AD on a terraced hillside; roughly a third survives, including the portico behind the stage and lateral staircases, now part of an archaeological park opened in 2003.

vici:vici-17567.desc: A small Roman theatre in the northeastern quarter of Meninx, the island of Jerba's Roman-period administrative centre and a major Mediterranean production site for purple (murex) dye.

vici:vici-17568.desc: A Roman circus/stadium in the northwestern quarter of Meninx, the island of Jerba's Roman-period administrative centre and a major Mediterranean production site for purple (murex) dye.

vici:vici-17570.desc: Roman theatre atop a hill at Althiburos (Kef region, Tunisia), c.60m in diameter with surviving arcaded galleries; under excavation and architectural study by a University of Palermo mission since 2007.

vici:vici-17606.desc: Julio-Claudian-era Roman theatre at Grumentum, a Lucanian municipium in Basilicata; partly excavated by Sestieri and Caputi in the 20th century and restored 1964-68 alongside the town's forum and amphitheatre.

vici:vici-17659.desc: Imperial-era Roman theatre of Aquae Statiellae (Acqui Terme), uncovered in the late 1990s near the Bollente thermal spring; some spectator seats were cut directly into the hillside, its stage now buried under modern buildings.

vici:vici-17668.desc: Neronian-era amphitheatre of Nuceria Alfaterna (Nocera Superiore), c.125x102m and among the largest in Campania, built after Nero banned spectacles at Pompeii following the AD 59 riot; rediscovered in 1929, still mostly buried.

vici:vici-17731.desc: Roman circus of Augusta Treverorum (Trier), a c.450m hippodrome monumentalized in the early 2nd century AD and depicted on the Polydus charioteer mosaic; no above-ground remains survive today.

vici:vici-17746.desc: Roofed concert hall built c.15 BC in the Athenian Agora, a gift of Marcus Agrippa; its two-story auditorium seated about 1,000 until the roof collapsed c.150 AD, later rebuilt with colossal Triton/Giant statues at its entrance.

vici:vici-17773.desc: Ancient Greek stadium at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidaurus, built mainly in the 4th-3rd century BC with stone seating for foot races, wrestling and boxing held every four years during the Asklepieia games.

vici:vici-17781.desc: Small Roman-period roofed odeon (c.30x39m) built within the courtyard of the Hellenistic Gymnasium at the Asklepieion of Epidaurus, dated to the late 2nd/early 3rd century AD and used for pantomime and singing contests.

vici:vici-17788.desc: Hellenistic stadium-gymnasium complex of Messene, built in the 3rd century BC and used into the 4th century AD; among the best-preserved ancient stadiums in Greece, with 18 rows of seats and Doric stoas housing the city's gymnasium.

vici:vici-17796.desc: Site of footraces and other athletic contests at the ancient Olympic Games from 776 BC; the track measures one Olympic stade (192.27 m) between marble starting and finish lines, with embankments once holding up to 45,000 spectators.

vici:vici-17809.desc: Roman-era odeon built in the 2nd century AD within the forum of Thessaloniki, on the site of an earlier bouleuterion; inscriptions and statues of the Muses found there confirm its use for musical contests and gladiatorial spectacles.

vici:vici-17815.desc: Roman theatre built near the sanctuary of Zeus at Dion in the 2nd century AD, reusing material from the older Hellenistic theatre it replaced after the city became Roman; excavated south-east of that earlier structure.

vici:vici-17830.desc: Stadium of the sanctuary of Poseidon at Isthmia, home of the Panhellenic Isthmian Games from 582 BC; two successive stadiums are attested, the later Hellenistic one fitted with a hysplex starting-gate mechanism for footraces.

vici:vici-17838.desc: Stadium of the sanctuary of Zeus at Nemea, built around 330 BC for the Nemean Games; athletes reached the track through a unique 36 m vaulted tunnel bearing ancient graffiti, including the victor's name Telestas.

vici:vici-17877.desc: Roman hippodrome of Berytus, discovered in 1988 in Beirut's city centre; covering roughly 3,500 m² it was the largest known hippodrome in the Roman Levant, laid out with a circuit resembling Rome's Circus Maximus.

vici:vici-17884.desc: Unique rectangular arena at Tyre's Al-Mina site, roughly 45 by 35 m with five rows of stone seating for about 2,000 spectators around a central pool; likely 4th-century AD in its present form, function unknown — possibly a water-sport venue.

vici:vici-17891.desc: A Roman theater built into the hillside south of Heliopolis's great sanctuary complex, with a parodos leading to the orchestra and stage; largely destroyed during 19th-century hotel construction, leaving only minimal remains today.

vici:vici-17900.desc: The larger of Gadara's two Roman theaters, built in black basalt into the north-eastern slope with an c.85m-wide cavea seating around 6,000; ruined by earthquake and later quarried for stone by the Ottoman-era village built over it.

vici:vici-17902.desc: Roman hippodrome of Gadara, a Decapolis city, used for equestrian spectacles; today only faint outlines survive west of the city's colonnaded street.

vici:vici-17970.desc: Dedicated in 13 BC by Lucius Cornelius Balbus with spoils from his African campaign, one of Rome's three permanent stone theaters (with those of Pompey and Marcellus); held over 11,000 spectators and was rebuilt after a fire under Domitian.

vici:vici-17982.desc: Circular island retreat within Hadrian's Villa, begun c.118 AD, ringed by a moat and portico with movable wooden bridges for total seclusion; the name 'Maritime Theatre' was coined in the 19th century by architect Blondel after its sea-themed friezes.

vici:vici-17986.desc: Roman theater of the 3rd century CE built directly over an earlier 4th-century BC Greek theater at Elea/Velia; its 47.5m cavea held roughly 3,000 spectators and faces the Tyrrhenian Sea below a Norman watchtower.

vici:vici-18116.desc: Theatre of Laodicea ad Mare (modern Latakia), a Seleucid-founded port that later became capital of Roman Syria under Septimius Severus; its cavea, built against the southeastern hill, measured c. 100 m across but survives only in traces.

vici:vici-18359.desc: Theatre of Zeugma, the Hellenistic-Roman city on the Euphrates named for its bridge of boats; excavated by Ankara University since 1987, the site is famed for its mosaics and was partly submerged when the Birecik Dam was completed in 2000.

vici:vici-18399.desc: Theatre of Seleucia on the Tigris, the dynastic capital founded by Seleucus I Nicator; the Hellenistic theatre stood on the town's south side beside a porticoed square and was uncovered by University of Michigan excavations (1927-1937) and later Italian missions.

vici:vici-18400.desc: Theatre/amphitheatre of Seleucia Pieria, Antioch's fortified port founded by Seleucus I Nicator; twenty-four tiers of benches partly survive, and Antiochus III camped nearby before his 219 BC assault that retook the city from Ptolemaic Egypt.

vici:vici-18566.desc: Hellenistic stadium of Arykanda (3rd-2nd century BC), unusually short at just over 100 m; the city was famed for its wrestlers, and a shrine with eight niches built into the seating was dedicated to their patron gods Heracles and Hermes.

vici:vici-18582.desc: The lesser of Hierapolis's two theatres, sited east of the Basilica Baths on the mountainside; unlike the city's well-preserved main theatre, it was almost completely destroyed by earthquakes, leaving only a few stairs.

vici:vici-18651.desc: Theatre of Aptera in Crete, built c. 3rd century BC and rebuilt in Roman style in the 1st century AD; damaged by a lime kiln erected in its cavea, it was excavated 2008-2015 and reopened in 2016 as Crete's only restored ancient theatre.

vici:vici-18701.desc: Theatre of Cenabum (Roman Orléans), built in the 1st century AD and enlarged in the 2nd, with a diameter exceeding 100 m and an orchestra c. 38 m across; unearthed in 1821 during quay construction, then demolished and its stone reused.

vici:vici-19259.desc: A small theatre/odeon within the Roman thermal bath complex at Montegrotto (ancient Aquae Patavinae), built from the later 1st century BC and used into the 3rd century AD; first uncovered in excavations of 1781–1788.

vici:vici-19265.desc: Roman theatre of Acelum (ancient Asolo), with seating for about 1,000; discovered in the late 19th century by local pharmacist Pacifico Scomazzetto and largely uncovered in excavations led by Guido Rosada, 1988–1993.

vici:vici-19321.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Ai-Khanoum (Alexandria on the Oxus) in Bactria, about 84 m in diameter with three royal loges and seating for 4,000–6,000; built under Seleucid/Greco-Bactrian rule and excavated by a French mission, 1965–1978.

vici:vici-19357.desc: Theatre of Aigai (Vergina), built alongside the royal palace in the later 4th century BC; here King Philip II of Macedon was assassinated by his bodyguard Pausanias in 336 BC during his daughter's wedding celebrations.

vici:vici-19371.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre at Amel-sur-l'Étang, forming a roughly 20-hectare suburban sanctuary complex with five temples and baths, revealed by aerial and geophysical surveys (2004–2011) near the neighboring Senon settlement.

vici:vici-19373.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre identified by aerial and geophysical survey at the Senon vicus (Meuse), part of a paired sanctuary agglomeration with the nearby Amel theatre-and-temples complex, occupied 1st–4th centuries AD.

vici:vici-19498.desc: Augustan-era theatre of Roman Corduba (built before c. 5 BC), the largest in Hispania and second only to Rome's Theatre of Marcellus in the empire, seating about 15,000; rediscovered in 1994 beneath the city's Archaeological Museum.

vici:vici-19606.desc: Roman theatre at Olba in Cilicia, built during the joint reign of emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus (161–169 AD) with seating for about 2,000, standing near the city's nymphaeum and Temple of Zeus.

vici:vici-19889.desc: Stadium built at Nicopolis for Augustus's Actian Games, inaugurated in 27 BC to mark his victory at Actium. Its two rounded ends (sphendonai) made it a transitional 'stadium-amphitheater' form, seating roughly 10,000.

vici:vici-19924.desc: A c. 50-metre Roman amphitheatre outside Diocletian's palace walls, built alongside the palace in the early 4th century AD; long mistaken for a theatre's seating, confirmed as an amphitheatre by 2013 excavations north of the old town.

vici:vici-19940.desc: Greek theatre cut into Cyrene's acropolis slope in the late 6th century BC, the oldest and largest of the city's five theatres, seating over 1,000; converted into a Roman amphitheatre in the 2nd century AD.

vici:vici-21686.desc: Roman theatre at the foot of Mount Berenice near Tiberias, built in the early 1st century AD close to the city's founding by Herod Antipas and enlarged in the 2nd century; seated over 7,000, excavated from 1990.

vici:vici-23250.desc: Theatre of the Arcadian city of Kleitor, in its southwestern quarter; already ruined and stripped of stone in Roman times, with its cavea and part of the seating only brought to light by recent excavation.

vici:vici-23252.desc: Roman Imperial-period theatre at Ambavri south of Kos town, about 108 m wide with capacity for up to 13,000, likely tied to festivals of the nearby Asklepieion; Hellenistic 'almond stone' seating hints at a 3rd-century-BC predecessor.

vici:vici-23293.desc: Theatre of ancient Oiniadai in Acarnania, first excavated in December 1900 by American archaeologist Benjamin Powell, who uncovered the stage, orchestra, and much of the cavea; fully cleared by Greek archaeologists in 1987.

vici:vici-23299.desc: Carian city near modern Vakıf in the Tavas district, on the Phrygia-Caria border; Christianised early, its bishop Polychronius attended the Council of Ephesus in 431 AD, and it later became a titular Catholic see.

vici:vici-23313.desc: Theatre of the Aeolian hilltop city of Aigai, excavated since 2004 by Ege University; its vomitorium (entrance passage) was uncovered in 2018 alongside the bouleuterion and odeon.

vici:vici-23561.desc: Large theatre of Alexandria Troas, built in the Greek tradition and embellished under Hadrian in the 2nd century AD; 2024 excavations estimated its capacity at roughly 12,000 spectators.

vici:vici-23820.desc: 2nd-century BC theatre at the healing sanctuary of Amphiaraos near Oropos; its Doric stage building survives along with five inscribed marble priests' thrones, seating about 300.

vici:vici-23987.desc: Roman theatre built into a hillside at Abila, one of the ten cities of the Decapolis; partially excavated since 1980 by John Brown University alongside a nymphaeum and Byzantine churches.

vici:vici-24006.desc: Theatre at Samaria-Sebaste with a semicircular, two-tier cavea built against the north slope during the Severan dynasty (early 3rd century AD), after Septimius Severus granted the city colonia status; excavated in the 1930s.

vici:vici-24023.desc: Monumental theatre-shaped stairway and colonnaded square in the sanctuary quarter of Pessinus, the Phrygian cult center of the mother goddess Cybele; excavated in the 1970s next to a Roman-period temple.

vici:vici-24175.desc: Stadium of Miletus, built in the first half of the 2nd century BC under Eumenes II of Pergamon; 191 m long with seating for roughly 15,000 on twelve tiers, and given a monumental colonnaded gate in the early 3rd century AD.

vici:vici-24210.desc: Ekklesiasterion (assembly-hall theatre) within the Asclepieion complex at Messene, first built in the late 3rd century BC for civic assemblies and performances honoring Messene and Asclepius; its proscenium and orchestra were repaired in the 1st and 3rd centuries AD.

vici:vici-24275.desc: Built on the north slope of Mount Pagos in the 3rd century BC and enlarged under Hadrian, Smyrna's theatre seated roughly 20,000 and hosted the provincial 'Asian Games'; abandoned in the 4th century AD and buried until excavations resumed in 2012-2016.

vici:vici-24291.desc: A small Roman-period theatre on the north edge of Toptepe Hill, the only visible survivor of Philadelphia (founded c. 159-138 BC by Attalus II of Pergamon), one of the Seven Churches of Revelation addressed by John.

vici:vici-24336.desc: A small Hellenistic theatre with about ten rows of seats at Apollonia in Lycia, a minor city that by the Roman period belonged to a sympoliteia led by Aperlae within the Lycian League.

vici:vici-24368.desc: An odeon on the acropolis of Selge, a rugged Pisidian mountain city on the Eurymedon river that struck its own coinage and, alongside its better-known theatre and stadium, hosted civic assembly and musical performances.

vici:vici-24426.desc: A roughly 10,000-seat stadium at Sillyon, built starting in the 1st century AD and used into the 5th century for athletic contests and festivals; only about a quarter has been excavated as of the 2020s.

vici:vici-24446.desc: A small south-facing theatre with 18 rows of seats at Kadyanda, dated to the late 2nd or early 1st century BC, part of a hilltop Lycian city discovered by Charles Fellows and identified by its native name Kadawanti.

vici:vici-24450.desc: A 1st-century AD stadium on the western edge of Sagalassos seating about 9,000 - far more than the city's own population - built to host athletic and gladiatorial events for the wider province of Pisidia.

vici:vici-24463.desc: A theatre, popularly nicknamed 'Colisseo' by locals, is the most conspicuous ruin of Heloros, a coastal Syracusan colony that stayed loyal to Hieron II until Rome retook it from Carthaginian sympathies in 214 BC.

vici:vici-24624.desc: Theatre of Iulia Concordia (modern Concordia Sagittaria), a Roman colony founded 42 BC where the Via Annia crossed the Via Postumia; its remains survive in Parco Signini alongside the forum and baths.

vici:vici-24654.desc: Amphitheatre of Roman Balsa (Luz de Tavira, Algarve), identified only through non-invasive geophysical survey of the largely unexcavated town; local inscriptions record residents funding naumachiae and boxing bouts.

vici:vici-24662.desc: Theatre of Felicitas Iulia Olisipo, built under Augustus and enlarged in AD 57 by the benefactor Caius Heius Primus; held roughly 4,000-5,000 spectators. Rediscovered in 1798, excavated from 1964, now the Museu do Teatro Romano.

vici:vici-24663.desc: Roman theatre of Bracara Augusta (Braga), the only one known in the northwestern Iberian Peninsula; found by chance in 1999 beside the Maximinos baths, with a cavea about 72.6 m across seating roughly 4,000-4,500.

vici:vici-24664.desc: Roman theatre of Bracara Augusta (Braga), the only one known in the northwestern Iberian Peninsula; found by chance in 1999 beside the Maximinos baths, with a cavea about 72.6 m across seating roughly 4,000-4,500.

vici:vici-24702.desc: One of two theatres at Kremna, a Pisidian hill city made the Roman colony Colonia Iulia Augusta Felix Cremnena after King Amyntas's death in 25 BC; the site later withstood the rebel Lydius's siege in the 270s AD.

vici:vici-24713.desc: Roman theatre of Bararus (Henchir Rougga) in Byzacena, sited across the wadi from the town's forum; a stage roughly 29x30 m is still traceable, though the structure is heavily ruined after stone-robbing and the earthquake of 365 CE that devastated the city.

vici:vici-24714.desc: Theatre of Thugga, built 168-169 CE under Antoninus Pius and personally funded by local magnate Publius Marcius Quadratus; its 19 tiers seated about 3,500 spectators, making it one of the best-preserved theatres of Roman Africa.

vici:vici-24718.desc: One of two Roman theatres at Utica, the Phoenician-founded city that briefly served as first capital of Africa Proconsularis after Carthage's fall; the site's Roman quarters were only uncovered by excavation starting in 1948-58.

vici:vici-24719.desc: One of two Roman theatres at Utica, the Phoenician-founded city that briefly served as first capital of Africa Proconsularis after Carthage's fall; the site's Roman quarters were only uncovered by excavation starting in 1948-58.

vici:vici-24720.desc: One of two Roman theatres at Utica, the Phoenician-founded city that briefly served as first capital of Africa Proconsularis after Carthage's fall; the site's Roman quarters were only uncovered by excavation starting in 1948-58.
