vici:vici-24722.desc: Roman theatre at Sicca Veneria (modern El Kef), a hill city on the road linking Carthage to Theveste, sited near the Casbah; the structure remains largely unpublished, with no established construction date or capacity.

vici:vici-24748.desc: The only fully excavated stadium on Cyprus, built near Kourion under Antoninus Pius in the 2nd century CE; a U-shaped track about 217 m long with seven rows of seating for c. 6,000 spectators, used for pentathlon events until the 4th century CE.

vici:vici-24756.desc: Theatre of Nea Paphos, cut into the bedrock of Fabrika hill and first built c. 300 BCE under the Ptolemies; monumentally expanded under the Antonines to seat up to 8,500, it stayed in use for over six centuries until earthquakes ended performances in the late 4th century CE.

vici:vici-24767.desc: Roman-period theater of Kydonia (ancient predecessor of Chania, Crete); the standing building was demolished in 1583 when Venetian authorities quarried its stone to reinforce the town's fortifications.

vici:vici-25175.desc: Roman-era theater of Mopsuestia (modern Misis/Yakapınar) on the Pyramus (Ceyhan) River, part of the city's public architecture alongside a stadium, colonnaded street, and the city's well-known Roman bridge; Mopsuestia prospered as a grain-exporting center after Pompey's annexation of Cilicia in 67 BC.

vici:vici-25185.desc: Site near Pozzuoli's former Marconi school, proposed by archaeologist Davide Peluso (2008) as the location of Puteoli's lost Roman theatre, based on the area's topography and nearby inscriptions of theatre-linked musicians' guilds (tibicines, scabillarii); unconfirmed and never excavated as such.

vici:vici-25212.desc: Theatre of the Peloponnesian city-state of Phlius, noted by Pausanias below the acropolis near temples of Asclepius and Demeter; excavations behind its stage building found twin stone foundations interpreted as supports for a mechane used for 'deus ex machina' stage entrances.

vici:vici-25517.desc: Hellenistic assembly hall of Morgantina in Sicily, a fan-shaped flight of monumental steps in the agora built for meetings of the citizen assembly (ekklesia), dated to the mid-3rd century BC and uncovered by the Princeton University expedition (1955-1968).

vici:vici-25533.desc: Rock-cut stadium of the Lycian hill city of Kadyanda, roughly 100 m long and 9-10 m wide with six surviving rows of seating on its north side, known from numerous inscriptions honoring victors of two local athletic festivals.

vici:vici-25556.desc: Bargylia, a Carian coastal city with a temple of Artemis Cindyas, sheltered Philip V of Macedon's fleet in 201/200 BC while it was blockaded there by Rhodian and Pergamene warships; its ruins include a theatre, defensive walls, and a palaestra.

vici:vici-25559.desc: Council house (bouleuterion) of Iasos in Caria, first built in the 4th century BC near the agora's south stoa; rebuilt in the 1st century AD with a three-quarter-circle cavea, functioning as a small covered theatre embellished under Hadrian.

vici:vici-25618.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Euromos in Caria, carved into a hillside and dating to about the mid-3rd century BC; originally seated roughly 2,000 spectators, though only five rows of seating survive above ground pending further excavation.

vici:vici-25709.desc: Theatre of ancient Corinth, built c. 400 BC and enlarged to seat up to 18,000; rebuilt under Rome and later used as a beast-fight arena, abandoned after Alaric's 396 AD sack. The nearby 'Erastus pavement' is linked by many scholars to the Erastus of Romans 16:23.

vici:vici-25758.desc: A Roman amphitheatre (200 x 90 m) raised at Patrae under Domitian in the late 1st century AD, reputedly marking the colony's centenary; it hosted the renowned Caesarea games of racing, pentathlon, boxing and wrestling.

vici:vici-25778.desc: Theatre of Ceryneia, an Achaean League city on a height above the Cerynites river; in myth the home of the Ceryneian Hind captured by Heracles, and a member state when the League revived around 280 BC.

vici:vici-25784.desc: Small theatre of Leontion excavated in 1958 by N. Gialouris on Kastritsi Hill, its nine surviving rows of seats built into the fortified Achaean city's defences; Leontion was destroyed by Philip V after 217 BC.

vici:vici-25903.desc: Roman theatre of Comana Cappadociae (Şar), the Cataonian temple-state sacred to the moon goddess Ma, visited by Strabo and Julius Caesar; excavated from 1967 alongside a monumental Roman gate and Byzantine church remains.

vici:vici-25987.desc: One of two theatres at Balboura, cut into the steep southern slope of the acropolis about 70 m above the valley; its cavea is unusually interrupted by a block of natural rock, and it remains unexcavated with no inscriptions found.

vici:vici-25989.desc: Balbura, in the Cabalian district of northern Lycia, had two theatres: this lower one lies in a natural hollow before the mountain south of the stream, opposite the upper theatre whose cavea is split by a rock outcrop.

vici:vici-25996.desc: Bubon's small sandstone theatre, at Dikmen Tepe near Ibecik in the Cabalian hills, preserved an inscribed letter of Commodus praising the city for repelling bandit raids and gaining a third vote in the Lycian League.

vici:vici-26013.desc: South of the stadium, Olympia's hippodrome staged four- and two-horse chariot and horse races from the 7th century BC; Pausanias described its starting gates and turning posts, but Alpheios river silt has buried all trace of it.

vici:vici-26035.desc: The small theatre of the fortified Attic deme of Rhamnous, near the sanctuary of Nemesis, doubled as public meeting space and lay within the settlement's extended 4th-century BC fortification circuit.

vici:vici-26053.desc: Cut into the acropolis slope of Arcadian Orchomenos around 300 BC, this 40-plus-row theatre held over 4,000 spectators for games honoring Dionysus; first excavated in 1913 by G. Blum and A. Plassart.

vici:vici-26099.desc: The odeon of Parion, the Hellespontine city made a Roman colony under Augustus, stands among the site's well-preserved Roman public buildings alongside its theatre and baths.

vici:vici-26152.desc: Nysa's bouleuterion, or gerontikon, in the northwest corner of the agora seated 600-700 people in a 12-row semicircular auditorium later reused as an odeon; recent scholarship dates the surviving building to the Antonine period, with no earlier phase.

vici:vici-26180.desc: Hellenistic stadium of the Illyrian city of Amantia, built on a terraced hillside in the first half of the 3rd century BC; c. 60m by 12.5m with 17 rows of seats on one side, holding about 4,000 spectators for footraces, boxing and discus.

vici:vici-26182.desc: Hellenistic theatre of the Illyrian city of Byllis, built in the 3rd century BC; about 78m across and 16m high with roughly 40 rows of seating, holding up to 7,500 spectators.

vici:vici-26261.desc: Roman theatre of Nicopolis, founded by Augustus after his 31 BC victory at Actium; hosted the quadrennial Actian Games and, per orchestra-barrier net-anchor holes and an inscribed invitation, was later adapted for gladiatorial and animal-hunt spectacles.

vici:vici-26289.desc: Roman-period theatral structure built around 3rd century AD around the altar of the Sanctuary of Artemis Orthia at Sparta, giving spectators seating to watch the ritual flogging (diamastigosis) of Spartan youths; excavated by the British School at Athens, 1906–10.

vici:vici-26364.desc: U-shaped stadium on the slope of Mount Pagos (Kadifekale) in ancient Smyrna, traditionally identified as the site where bishop Polycarp was martyred by Proconsul Statius Quadratus in the mid-2nd century AD.

vici:vici-26435.desc: Small Roman-period theatre of the Lycian hill town of Sidyma, described by 19th-century explorer Charles Fellows as diminutive but finely built; the site has never been officially excavated.

vici:vici-26610.desc: Roman-period odeon (small roofed hall for music and oratory) in the agora of ancient Thasos, uncovered during French School at Athens excavations of the agora that began in 1911.

vici:vici-26778.desc: Theatre in the eastern quarter of Notion (New Colophon), near the North Gate; first noted in the early 20th century, investigated by Erol Atalay in the 1980s and surveyed by a University of Michigan–Brown University team in 2014–2018.

vici:vici-26782.desc: Small rock-cut theatre or odeon (about 32 m across, 11 seat rows) at the foot of the Panionion hill on Mt Mycale, presumed to be the bouleuterion where the twelve-city Ionian League met for the Panionia festival of Poseidon Helikonios.

vici:vici-26837.desc: Frescoed reception hall of the Roman theatre at Metropolis in Ionia, paved with a mosaic (its figures debated as either a Four Seasons allegory or Dionysiac scene), uncovered during excavations running since 1989.

vici:vici-27446.desc: 2nd-century AD amphitheatre outside the walls of Tusculum, roughly 80 x 53 m with a capacity of about 3,000; its entrances were excavated in 1820 and the rest in 1867.

vici:vici-27607.desc: Roman theatre at Durovernum Cantiacorum (Canterbury): an earthen-banked timber theatre built under the Flavians (AD 69-96) was replaced around AD 220 by a stone theatre seating roughly 3,000, one of the largest in Britain.

vici:vici-27806.desc: The only confirmed Roman circus (chariot-racing track) in Britain, built in the early 2nd century AD, possibly under Hadrian, south of the Colchester colonia; about 450 m long with eight starting gates and seating for 8,000+, discovered in 2004-05 at the former Colchester Garrison.

vici:vici-27807.desc: Roman theatre at the Gosbecks religious complex near Colchester (Camulodunum), Britain's largest at about 82 m diameter and c.5,000 seats; a timber theatre was replaced by one of turf, clay and stone revetment, demolished by the mid-3rd century.

vici:vici-27961.desc: A Roman-period council house on a terrace east of Knidos's Harbor Street; a raised central podium and traces of a colonnaded, timber-roofed hall identify it as the city's bouleuterion.

vici:vici-27979.desc: One of two theatres at Knidos, cut into the hillside above the harbor; the excavated, harbor-side theatre held roughly 5,000 spectators, with a temple of Dionysos standing nearby.

vici:vici-28127.desc: Site of ancient Perperene, an Aeolian colony in Mysia on the Kozak plateau near Pergamon, noted for copper mines and vineyards; later renamed Theodosiopolis and became a Christian bishopric.

vici:vici-28392.desc: No hippodrome has actually been located at Nemea; its existence and the lengths of its horse and four-horse chariot races are known only from ancient literary sources, not excavation.

vici:vici-28395.desc: Early stadium of the Panhellenic sanctuary of Poseidon at Isthmia, built around 550 BC with a stone-and-rope starting mechanism (hysplex); excavated by Oscar Broneer from 1952, it hosted the biennial Isthmian Games.

vici:vici-28465.desc: One of the civic assembly buildings on Monte Iato, the Hellenistic-Roman hill city of ancient Ietas in western Sicily, excavated by the University of Zurich since 1971.

vici:vici-28578.desc: Theatre of ancient Mylasa, Caria's largest city; built on the east slope of Topbaşı Hill, it survives only as an eroded hollow in the bedrock, with no seating visibly preserved today.

vici:vici-28630.desc: A small, hillside-cut odeum among the acropolis ruins of Bargylia, a Carian coastal city near modern Boğaziçi; the site is privately owned and unexcavated, so the structure survives only as surface remains.

vici:vici-28677.desc: Carian city founded (or renamed) by the Hecatomnid dynast Mausolus in the mid-4th century BC via synoecism of the Lelegian town of Syangela; subjected to Halicarnassus after Alexander's conquest, and birthplace of the historian Philippus of Theangela.

vici:vici-28743.desc: Theatre built into the hillside within the sanctuary of the healing demigoddess Hemithea (Molpadia) at Kastabos, used for her festival, the Kastabeia; the sanctuary's main buildings date to c.300 BC and were excavated by J.M. Cook and W.H. Plommer from 1960.

vici:vici-28777.desc: Carian sanctuary city of Zeus, enclosed by massive isodomic fortification walls; excavated and published by Louis and Jeanne Robert, who recorded inscriptions including a 351/341 BC dedication by the Hecatomnid Idrieus and a 203/190 BC letter from the Seleucid official Zeuxis.

vici:vici-28822.desc: Roman-period theatre on Colossae's eastern slope, estimated to have seated around 5,000; the Phrygian city is best known as recipient of the Apostle Paul's Epistle to the Colossians. Systematic excavation began only in 2021, led by Pamukkale University.

vici:vici-28847.desc: Aeolian coastal city whose theatre has been located northwest of Beriki Tepe hill, though little survives visibly; excavations in 1880-82 uncovered roughly 4,000 Hellenistic-period tombs with terracotta figurines, most now in the Louvre.

vici:vici-28850.desc: Well-preserved Hellenistic theatre at the fortified hilltop settlement known as the "Castle of Veliani" (identified as ancient Elaea) near Chrysavgi in Thesprotia, above the Acheron-Kokytos valley, alongside an adjacent Hellenistic arcade and semi-underground storage rooms.

vici:vici-28854.desc: Aeolian city founded by colonists from Lesbos, notable for its two harbours and as birthplace of the philosopher Arcesilaus, founder of the Middle Academy; theatre and stadium remains survive, and the town suffered a severe earthquake under Titus.

vici:vici-28864.desc: Ionian city and birthplace of the philosopher Anaxagoras; after relocating from the mainland to an offshore island during the Ionian Revolt, it built a north-facing theatre there alongside a paved street and a temple.

vici:vici-28891.desc: Roman theatre of Florentia, built beneath what is now Palazzo Vecchio in the 1st century AD; excavated 1876–2010, its cavea held an estimated 10,000–15,000 spectators, comparable to Rome's Theatre of Marcellus.

vici:vici-28915.desc: Bouleuterion of the Ionian city of Notion, one of the largest known Hellenistic council houses (c. 30.5 x 24.3 m, seating ~500); excavated by the University of Michigan from 2023 after prior illicit digging.

vici:vici-28981.desc: Marketplace (macellum) at Sebatum, a Roman road station on the Via Julia Augusta near modern St. Lorenzen in South Tyrol, where the settlement's weekly nundinae (markets) were held; now the Mansio Sebatum museum site.

vici:vici-29006.desc: Roman theatre at Sebaste (Samaria), built by Herod the Great after Augustus granted him the city in 27 BC; part of Herod's Augustan forum complex, it could seat up to about 6,000 spectators.

vici:vici-29249.desc: Amphitheatre of Aquinum, a Roman colony traditionally considered the birthplace of the poet Juvenal; only scant remains survive within the walled site alongside temples, a basilica and the Porta Capuana gate.

vici:vici-29257.desc: Bouleuterion (also used as an odeon) in the agora of Seleucia/Lyrbe in Pamphylia, a six-row council chamber that hosted both civic assembly meetings and musical performances.

vici:vici-29316.desc: Amphitheatre in the harbour district of Roman Terracina, an imperial-period building of which little survives; a marble bust of Sophocles found nearby in 1838 is now in the Vatican Museums.

vici:vici-29358.desc: Selge's Roman-era stadium, cut into the hillside with a U-shaped track and a single sphendone; only a few tiers of seating survive from a renovation programme that also rebuilt the city's agora and theatre.

vici:vici-29362.desc: The starting-gate tower of Mediolanum's circus, built for Maximian around 293–305 AD as part of Milan's imperial building programme; later converted into the bell tower of the church of San Maurizio, now within the Archaeological Museum.

vici:vici-29423.desc: A small Hellenistic theatre at Issus, identified with Kinet Höyük near İskenderun, site of Alexander the Great's decisive 333 BC victory over Darius III; excavated during the Bilkent University campaigns of 1992–2012.

vici:vici-29764.desc: A Roman-era odeon at Ascalon (Tel Ashkelon) built mainly for musical performances, excavated alongside Herod's basilica and restored during the Israel Nature and Parks Authority's park renovation begun in 2008.

vici:vici-29785.desc: A Phoenician stadium at Amrit carved from bedrock, about 230 m long with seven rows of seats partly preserved; dated to the 4th–3rd century BC, it is among the oldest stadiums known, likely used for sacred and funerary games.

vici:vici-29799.desc: The ruined odeon of Pinara, one of the six chief cities of the Lycian League (three votes), stands near the site entrance; the city's better-preserved theatre lies nearby.

vici:vici-29803.desc: The Sphendone, curved southern end of the Hippodrome of Constantinople, rests on 25 vaulted arches that leveled the track toward the sea slope; its remains, excavated by Stanley Casson's British team in 1927, are the hippodrome's most substantial survival.

vici:vici-29810.desc: A 2nd-century AD Roman theatre at Bizye (modern Vize), the only theatre known in ancient Thrace, with marble seating for about 4,000; excavated in 1998 and further studied in 2003, its finds are held in Kırklareli Museum.

vici:vici-29835.desc: A Hellenistic Greek theatre cut into Apollonia's hillside north of the Agora, buttressed by an artificial bank; now under excavation by a joint German-Albanian team, it fell from use in late antiquity when a church was built over its reused seating blocks.

vici:vici-29836.desc: Small odeon on the north side of Apollonia's Agora, built around the mid-2nd century AD with sixteen rows of seats for roughly 300 spectators and a shrine faced by two Ionic columns; excavated 1978, restored 1984-87.

vici:vici-29842.desc: Theatre built at Oricum by the 2nd-century AD senator and benefactor Herodes Atticus, later destroyed by an earthquake; a nearby monumental fountain (nymphaeum) used as a rainwater cistern was long mistaken for a second theatre.

vici:vici-29881.desc: Small Hellenistic-style theatre with only seven or eight rows of seating, built into the northwest slope of Idebessos's acropolis hill; the city has never been excavated, known only from Akdeniz University surveys in 2000 and 2008.

vici:vici-29912.desc: Roman theatre of Helvia Recina, the largest in the Picenum region, which flourished under Augustus and Tiberius; its 71.8m cavea is the only above-ground remnant of the city and today hosts open-air opera performances.

vici:vici-29913.desc: Roman theatre of Urbs Salvia, built in 23 AD in the Hellenistic tradition on a hillside terrace above the forum; one of the largest theatres in ancient Italy, with a three-tier cavea and colonnaded stage backdrop.

vici:vici-30009.desc: Theatre on Zambaktepe's slopes above Lake Uluabat at Apollonia ad Rhyndacum, founded in the late 4th century BC with Hellenistic and Roman building phases; seated about 6,000 in an 80m cavea; excavation since 2021 uncovered an inscribed reserved seat naming a priestess.

vici:vici-30088.desc: One of the largest Roman theatres in Spain, partly carved into the sandstone hillside of Tiermes (Termantia), a Celtiberian hillfort allied with Numantia that became a Roman municipium in 98 BCE.

vici:vici-30154.desc: Theatre of Magarsus, the port-sanctuary of Mallos where Alexander the Great sacrificed to Athena Magarsis before Issus; excavations uncovered a c. 30m-wide, 150m-long theatre seating roughly 3,000, with a rare sea view.

vici:vici-30167.desc: Small, still-unexcavated theatre (estimated capacity c. 2,000) on a hillside at Prymnessos, a Phrygian town near Sülün that later served as a Christian bishopric attested at councils from 381 to 787 CE.

vici:vici-30170.desc: Theatre of Astra, a walled Roman-Byzantine town in Isauria near Tamaşalık whose identity was confirmed by inscriptions first recorded by Sterrett in 1885; rescue excavations there also uncovered a necropolis and church.

vici:vici-30173.desc: Small theatre/odeion at Colybrassus, a Cilician hill settlement 1,000m up on Susuz Dağ that also held a Temple of Hercules; the city became a Byzantine bishopric before vanishing from record after 1199 CE.

vici:vici-30176.desc: Perperene was an Aeolian colony of ancient Mysia on the Kozak plateau near Bergama, known in antiquity for its copper mines and vineyards; specific details of its theatre are not attested.

vici:vici-30195.desc: Gigthis (Bou Ghrara), a Phoenician trading post absorbed by Rome after Thapsus in 46 BCE and later a municipium under Antoninus Pius; its forum, arch, and temples were cleared by French excavators from 1901.

vici:vici-30379.desc: Carian city founded in the 6th century BCE on the Harpasus river, mentioned by Ptolemy, Stephanus of Byzantium, and Pliny; its late Hellenistic-to-early-Imperial theatre and city walls survive near Esenköy in the Nazilli district.

vici:vici-31066.desc: Small theatre (capacity roughly 1,000) built into a hillside at the Birketein sanctuary north of Gerasa, part of a 2nd–3rd century AD complex with twin pools and baths; the site hosted the nocturnal Maiuma festival of Dionysus in late antiquity.

vici:vici-31073.desc: The council house (bouleuterion) of Assos, a square building roughly 21x21m with columns in antis, built in the late 4th century BC by the local benefactor Ladomos and his wife — one of the oldest bouleuteria known in Asia Minor.

vici:vici-31101.desc: Theatre of Thelepte, a Roman colonia in Byzacena near modern Fériana; the city became seat of the province's military governor in the 6th century and was refortified under Justinian, per Procopius.

vici:vici-31121.desc: Ruined theatre of Aggar (Henchir Sidi Amara) in the Siliana plain, identified with the ancient town by L. Ladjimi-Sebaï; some cavea seating steps survive. The site also preserves a triumphal arch, forum/capitol, and a Byzantine fort built atop a temple of Juno.

vici:vici-31123.desc: Small theatre of Seressi, a Roman town near modern El Fahs, standing alongside the settlement's triumphal arch.

vici:vici-31202.desc: Theatre associated with Thunigaba, a Roman-Berber town near modern Aïn Laabed in the Nefza region that later became a bishopric; no remains are currently visible above ground.

vici:vici-31205.desc: Amphitheatre of the Roman colonia Thuburbo Minus (Tebourba), partly cut into a hillside and measuring roughly 36x48m, built in the 2nd century AD; one of only three Tunisian amphitheatres, with Uthina and El Jem, known to have underground service galleries. Still standing in the 17th century, it was later demolished for bridge material.

vici:vici-31274.desc: The only surviving public monument of the Vestinian town of Aveia, an early imperial theater built on the middle terrace of the settlement, unusually located outside the city walls.

vici:vici-31276.desc: Built in the 1st century BC on the Colle del Girfalco acropolis, this theater held roughly 2,000 spectators; its buried remains, studied in 1786, 1853, and 1934, lie beneath the church of Madonna del Carmine.

vici:vici-31279.desc: Rediscovered in 1943 and excavated from 2015, the theater of Urvinum Mataurense (1st c. BC-1st c. AD) stood out for its lavish marble revetment sourced from quarries across the Mediterranean, from Carrara to Egypt.

vici:vici-31280.desc: Probably built under Augustus as part of his urban development of Ariminum, the theater had an external diameter of about 80 meters and a 23-meter-wide stage; its remains are now embedded in later buildings.

vici:vici-31281.desc: Theater of the Roman town of Mevaniola, dated to the early 1st century BC; its perfectly circular orchestra follows Greek-Hellenistic rather than standard Roman design. Excavated in the 1950s-60s after the site's 1934 discovery.

vici:vici-31282.desc: Begun around 88 BC as part of Bononia's transition to a Roman municipium, this was the first stone theater in Roman architecture, predating Pompey's theater in Rome by decades. Discovered in 1977 during restoration on via Carbonesi.

vici:vici-31283.desc: Built in the northwestern part of ancient Luca near the city walls, probably in the early Augustan period; the semicircular cavea had a diameter of about 78 meters, with traces surviving at the Church of Sant'Agostino.

vici:vici-31287.desc: Part of the villa where Augustus exiled his grandson Agrippa Postumus in 6 AD; the small theatre, alongside a thermal bath and mosaic-floored villa, was uncovered by abbot Gaetano Chierici in the 19th century.

vici:vici-31288.desc: The council house of the Roman colony of Cosa (founded 273 BC), excavated by the American Academy in Rome under Frank E. Brown; the original timber-and-stone curia was rebuilt as a three-part hall around 173 BC.

vici:vici-31290.desc: Asisium's theatre is known only from two inscriptions found near Santa Maria Maggiore naming the gens Propertia as builders, possibly linking it to the poet Sextus Propertius; its physical remains have never been located.

vici:vici-31291.desc: Part of a public sacred precinct with an amphitheatre and temple below the later Villa Fidelia, on the road from Porta Venere toward Assisi; demolished by the early 20th century, leaving only a buried pillar core visible today.

vici:vici-31292.desc: Built in the 1st century AD against the hillside above the Via Flaminia; its curved cavea foundations and two supporting ambulatories survive embedded in later houses, one now the Redibis restaurant.

vici:vici-31293.desc: Ancient Tuder's Roman theatre was partly overbuilt by the early medieval circuit walls; a 2012 archaeological reassessment (Chiaraluce) reinterpreted the structural relationship between the theatre remains and the later fortifications.

vici:vici-31294.desc: Distinct from the city's amphitheatre, this 1st-century-BC theatre stood near the forum on the Via Flaminia crossing; only fragmentary opus reticulatum walls and one vaulted lateral entrance survive beneath later buildings.

vici:vici-31295.desc: Excavated in 1829, when Augustan-era statues of Livia and her grandsons Gaius and Lucius Caesar were recovered from it; recent ground-penetrating radar survey has since mapped its full structural plan beneath the abandoned city.

vici:vici-31300.desc: The Samnite-Roman town of Cluviae's theatre, built in opus reticulatum at Piano Laroma and dated to the 1st century BC-1st century AD, had a cavea front over 36m long and a rectangular scaenae building of 24x12m.

vici:vici-31303.desc: Built in the first half of the 1st century AD with a diameter of about 75m, Corfinium's theatre survives mainly through inscriptions recording its construction and a guild of 23 scabillarii dancers; little remains beneath Piazza del Teatro.

vici:vici-31304.desc: Carved into the slope of Colle Pettorino in two building phases from the late 2nd to mid-1st century BC, the theatre was later restored, reportedly under the usurper Magnentius, and abandoned after Alba Fucens declined post a 6th-century earthquake.

vici:vici-31305.desc: Part of the Republican-era Sanctuary of Hercules Victor (c.120-82 BC), the theatre had three vomitoria, a scaenae frons and an orchestra pit, and abutted the sanctuary's underground Via Tecta road; restored in 2008-09.

vici:vici-31307.desc: A small court theatre with an oval cavea near Villa Adriana's entrance, long quarried for building stone; 18th-century digs by Count Fede found paired herms of Tragedy and Comedy, later restored by Cavaceppi and now in the Vatican Museums.

vici:vici-31310.desc: Part of the sanctuary of Juno Gabina at ancient Gabii: a sacred precinct cut partly into rock, with porticoes, shops, and a theater on its south side facing the late 2nd-century BC temple of Juno.

vici:vici-31311.desc: A concert/oratory hall built by Domitian on the Campus Martius, seating up to c.11,000, restored by Apollodorus of Damascus around AD 106; its cavea outline survives in the curved facade of Palazzo Massimo alle Colonne.

vici:vici-31313.desc: A small amphitheater within the Villa of the Quintilii on the Via Appia, seized by Commodus in AD 182 after he had the villa's owners executed; tradition holds he trained and hunted beasts here as a gladiator.

vici:vici-31314.desc: The c.500m hippodrome-shaped garden of the Villa of the Quintilii, expanded under Commodus after his AD 182 seizure of the estate; a porticoed racetrack-garden used for chariot exercise and imperial promenades, lined with statuary and fountains.

vici:vici-31315.desc: The private theater of Domitian's Alban Villa, built c. AD 81-96 on the middle terrace above Lake Albano; excavated fragments are held in the Antiquarium of Castel Gandolfo, with ruins still visible in the Villa Barberini grounds.

vici:vici-31316.desc: An elliptical amphitheater (c.56m long) confirmed by 2012-13 excavations as part of the imperial 'Villa degli Antonini' estate at ancient Lanuvium; ancient sources say Commodus earned his nickname 'Roman Hercules' here by killing wild beasts.

vici:vici-31317.desc: Remains of Lanuvium's Roman theater and its city walls survive in the modern town, near a porticoed precinct in opus reticulatum associated with the city's famed temple of Juno Sospita.

vici:vici-31319.desc: The topmost terrace of the late 2nd-century BC Sanctuary of Fortuna Primigenia formed a theater-like cavea crowned by a small circular tholos, reached via the 'Hemicycles' terrace with its oracular sortes well.

vici:vici-31322.desc: Built in the early Augustan period on the slopes of Monte Santa Croce, the theatre had a roughly 60m stage front and seated about 3,500; an inscription credits a member of the gens Vibia with funding it. Abandoned after the destructive 346 CE earthquake.

vici:vici-31324.desc: Theatre of the Samnite hilltop town identified as Callifae, with a masonry stage and a cavea cut directly into the rock following the terrain — only the second rock-cut Samnite theatre found after Pietrabbondante. The settlement was destroyed after the Social War (88 BC).

vici:vici-31329.desc: Theatre of Allifae, built inside the walls near today's Piazza del Vescovato; recorded still standing with two arcades in the 18th century before being demolished in 1836 for cathedral stone. Fragments including marble portrait heads re-emerged in 1965 and 1989.

vici:vici-31330.desc: Theatre of the Samnite-Roman town of Trebula Balliensis, excavated in its southwestern quarter, where statues and public inscriptions (now largely in the Museo Campano, Capua) suggest the town's forum also stood. The Roman-era settlement developed here from the 2nd century BC.

vici:vici-31332.desc: A theatre known only from inscriptions at ancient Telesia (Telese Vetere/San Salvatore Telesino), recorded as roughly 48.7m in diameter with a 37.7m orchestra; despite this epigraphic evidence, excavation has never located its physical remains.

vici:vici-31333.desc: Augustan-era theatre in Formia's Castellone district, locally called 'Gliu Canciegl'; its cavea faced south toward the sea and its stage was about 24m long. Tradition places the martyrdom of St Erasmus here in 303 CE; the ruins are now built over by a residential quarter.

vici:vici-31336.desc: Capua's Republican-era amphitheatre (130-90 BC), sited about 20m from its later Flavian successor and holding an estimated 20,000 spectators; likely the site of the gladiatorial school from which Spartacus's revolt broke out in 73 BC. Demolished to build the standing amphitheatre.

vici:vici-31338.desc: Scholars believe the 18th-century Casina Spinelli hunting lodge was built directly over the remains of Suessula's Roman theatre; excavations beneath and around it exposed part of the ancient city's forum.

vici:vici-31339.desc: Elliptical opus reticulatum/opus mixtum walls of a 1st-century AD theatre were found in 1982 under the stables of Acerra's Castello dei Conti; inscriptions record Gnaeus Stennius Egnatius, priest of Isis and Serapis, funding a day of games.

vici:vici-31341.desc: A 2nd-century AD theatre built against the hillside at Punta Sarparella served the sailors of the Classis Misenensis, Rome's principal imperial fleet based at Misenum; bradyseism has submerged part of it below sea level.

vici:vici-31344.desc: A small Roman theatre was uncovered in 1855 beneath the terrace of Sorrento's Hotel Victoria, alongside a long wall thought to belong to an imperial-era residence, with a rock-cut tunnel descending toward the sea.

vici:vici-31351.desc: A rock-cut and sandstone-block semicircular theatre seating roughly 200 was built into the hillside of this Brettian hilltop settlement (4th century BC), tentatively identified with Cossa, and served as a communal meeting place.

vici:vici-31355.desc: The Teatro Berga, Vicetia's Roman theatre, was built in the 1st century BC with a cavea about 82 m across seating over 5,000; adorned with marble statues of the Julio-Claudian family, it was surveyed by Andrea Palladio before being built over in the 17th century.

vici:vici-31356.desc: The smaller of Altinum's two Roman theatres, identified as an odeon through geophysical resistivity survey within the city's monumental centre; unlike its larger neighbour it had not been excavated as of 2026.

vici:vici-31357.desc: The larger of Altinum's two Roman theatres, first mapped by geophysical prospection; a 2025-26 excavation campaign uncovered sections of its cavea walls near the city's forum and the Via Annia.

vici:vici-31358.desc: Remains of a Roman theatre at Eporedia, distinct from the town's better-known amphitheatre, first catalogued by Carlo Promis in his 1883 study of its surviving traces; little is visible above ground today.

vici:vici-31361.desc: A theatre proposed for Roman Palma (Palmaria) in a 1997 architectural hypothesis applying Vitruvian design criteria to the city's ancient street grid; unlike the excavated theatre at Pollentia in northern Mallorca, no dig has confirmed the structure.

vici:vici-31362.desc: An unexcavated theatre cavity at Akmonia (Ahat Köyü, Uşak province), a Phrygian city mentioned by Cicero; visible remains include two temple podiums and gymnasium foundations alongside the theatre pit, with no standing structure surviving.

vici:vici-31364.desc: U-shaped stadium of Claudiopolis (Bithynium), built in the 2nd century AD, probably under Hadrian; inscriptions naming priests Secundus and Anatole record gladiatorial games held there. Originally ~190m long; excavated since 2008, now the best-preserved Roman structure in Bolu.

vici:vici-31365.desc: Roman theater of Nicomedia, capital of the Roman Empire under Diocletian from 284 AD; reportedly among antiquity's largest theaters, it lies mostly unexcavated beneath İzmit's Orhan neighborhood, exposed by rubble-clearance after the 1999 earthquake.

vici:vici-31371.desc: Bath-gymnasium complex built in the 2nd century AD by Publius Vedius Antoninus and his wife Flavia Pappiana, dedicated to Artemis and Antoninus Pius; its 70x30m palaestra beside the Great Theatre is thought to have trained actors.

vici:vici-31392.desc: At the Roman civitas of Murgi (Ciavieja, El Ejido), georadar survey detected a curved wall interpreted as a possible amphitheatre, supported by a 2nd-century inscription honoring Porcia Maura referencing circus games; identification remains provisional.

vici:vici-31393.desc: Remains of the Roman theatre of Castulo survive near the acropolis of this Iberian-Roman city by Linares (Jaén), part of a public complex with baths, forum and an amphitheatre built as Castulo adopted the Roman urban model.

vici:vici-31394.desc: Roman circus of Laminium (Alhambra, Ciudad Real), identified in the late 1990s during roadworks on the N-340; a rectangular structure with a central longitudinal wall interpreted as the spina, now buried beneath the road.

vici:vici-31396.desc: Theatre of the Celtiberian-Roman hilltop city Arcóbriga (Cerro Villar, Monreal de Ariza), built on the slope between the acropolis and the public-buildings plateau; the site was excavated by the Marquis of Cerralbo in the early 20th century.

vici:vici-31397.desc: Theatre of Bílbilis, birthplace of the poet Martial, linked to the forum by porticoes and stairs faced with marble from Carrara and Chemtou; a two-story scaenae frons with Corinthian capitals gave the venue a capacity near 4,500.

vici:vici-31398.desc: Rock-cut theatre of Colonia Clunia Sulpicia, built under Emperor Tiberius with capacity for c. 10,000 spectators — one of the largest in Hispania — remodeled in the 2nd century for amphitheatre-style spectacles.

vici:vici-31399.desc: Amphitheatre of Lucus Augusti (Lugo), built outside the city walls near a gladiatorial palaestra and the temples of Isis and Venus; its modest size (c. 2,800 capacity) matched the town's population.

vici:vici-31400.desc: Legionary amphitheatre of Legio (León), one of only five known legionary amphitheatres in the Roman Empire; begun by Legio VI Victrix in the 1st century and enlarged by Legio VII Gemina to seat up to 8,000.

vici:vici-31401.desc: Roman theatre of the colonia Iulia Constantia Zilil (Dchar Jdid, near Asilah), sited just outside the west gate of the 3rd-century town wall; today only the cavea depression and stage-wall foundations survive.

vici:vici-31402.desc: Roman circus of Sitifis (Sétif), a chariot-racing track roughly 450m long and 70m wide, likely built or restored in late antiquity; largely built over today, with only its curved southern end still visible.

vici:vici-31404.desc: Small 1st-century AD theatre or odeion (23m diameter) beside the Asclepieion of ancient Lissos, likely used for performances tied to the sanctuary's healing cult.

vici:vici-31405.desc: Roman theatre of Lyttos (Lyktos), described by 16th-century drawings and recent excavation as the largest theatre on Crete; nearby a civic hall dedicated to Hadrian was completed in 118 AD.

vici:vici-31406.desc: Rock-cut theatral area of the Doric city of Lato, with stone benches for about 350 spectators beside the agora, used for civic assembly as much as performance.

vici:vici-31410.desc: A stepped, uncurved stone seating area of about ten rows (21-29 m long) set into the hillside beside the 4th-century BC temple of Despoina at Lycosura, likely for spectators of the goddess's mystery rites; the sanctuary held Damophon's colossal cult statue group, found in 1889.

vici:vici-31414.desc: The only substantially preserved hippodrome from Greek antiquity (roughly 250 x 50 m), built after the 7th century BC in the Lower Sanctuary of Zeus on Mount Lykaion, where the quadrennial Lykaia games staged chariot and horse races into the 3rd century BC.

vici:vici-31416.desc: A 4th-century BC theatre (its stage rebuilt around 245 BC) attached to the fortified hill-city of Typaneae in Triphylia, one of only two known ancient theatres in Elis Prefecture; part of its cavea, retaining wall, and a proedria throne survive in place.

vici:vici-31417.desc: Theatre of Elis, built mainly in Hellenistic times on the acropolis slope near a temple of Dionysus that housed a statue by Praxiteles (Pausanias); excavated by the Austrian Archaeological Institute in 1910-1914 and again from 1960, with an estimated capacity of 5,000-8,000.

vici:vici-31418.desc: A theatre at Psophis in northwestern Arcadia, near the confluence of the Erymanthos and Aroanios rivers; noted by 19th-century traveller William Martin Leake on a hillside but, despite being a modest settlement described by Pausanias, it has not been securely relocated by later excavation.

vici:vici-31425.desc: A small 4th-3rd century BC theatre at the foot of the fortified hill of ancient Macynia in Aetolia, with an unusual layout combining a 14-row curved cavea and an adjoining 3-row straight wing forming a single-row proedria, built using the Doric-foot module.

vici:vici-31426.desc: Theatre of New Pleuron in Aetolia, built around 230 BC contemporary with the city walls after Demetrios II of Macedon destroyed old Pleuron in 234 BC; a well-preserved five-section cavea with six staircases and a six-columned proscenium abut the city wall.

vici:vici-31429.desc: Theatre of ancient Leucas, built c. 4th century BC on Koulmos Hill; rediscovered in 1997 and systematically excavated from 2015, with 21 seat rows for c. 3,500 spectators and carved marble thrones.

vici:vici-31430.desc: Roman theatre of Nikopolis, built in the early 1st century AD as part of Augustus's building program for the city founded after Actium; capacity c. 5,000, renovated under Hadrian in the 2nd century AD.

vici:vici-31431.desc: Roman odeon at Nikopolis, built in the 1st century AD with its cavea carried on three semicircular stoas since the ground lacked a natural slope; remained in use until the later 3rd century AD.

vici:vici-31432.desc: Small theatre-odeon-bouleuterion of Ambracia, capital of Pyrrhus of Epirus, built late 4th-early 3rd century BC over an earlier bathhouse; the smallest known Greek theatre, seating only about 500.

vici:vici-31438.desc: Theatre of Gitana, capital of the Thesprotian League, built mid-3rd century BC and destroyed by Rome in 167 BC; 65m diameter with 28 seat rows, many inscribed with acts of manumission.

vici:vici-31439.desc: Ancient theatre of Tanagra, a Boeotian city famed for its Geometric-to-Hellenistic cemetery and Tanagra terracotta figurines; the theatre remains only partly uncovered, cut through by a later water-supply channel.

vici:vici-31443.desc: Theatre of Demetrias, built with the city's founding by Demetrios Poliorketes in 294-292 BC; a Hellenistic cavea and stage over 24m across, seating upward of 2,500, excavated from 1907 onward.

vici:vici-31445.desc: Theatre of Thessalian Scotussa, built into a natural hollow inside the city's southwest wall; the polis, home of Olympic pankratiast Polydamas, lay near the 197 BC Battle of Cynoscephalae and is under excavation by a Greek-Italian team since 2014.

vici:vici-31448.desc: A council hall built within Dion's Great Thermae bath complex in the later 2nd century AD; the roughly 400-seat odeon-style room hosted musical and poetic performances and likely doubled as the city's bouleuterion (assembly hall).

vici:vici-31453.desc: Theatre of Palaiopoli, the fortified Classical-Hellenistic capital of ancient Andros founded in the 7th-6th century BC on the island's west coast; only fragmentary remains survive, and the site today hosts summer performances of ancient Greek drama.

vici:vici-31456.desc: Track and seating built in Delos's sanctuary of Apollo in the early 3rd century BC for the athletic contests of the Delia festival; at roughly 270 m it is among the smallest stadia known from ancient Greece.

vici:vici-31457.desc: Theatre of Ancient Thera, the Dorian city on Mesa Vouno founded by Theras of Sparta in the 9th century BC; excavated by Friedrich Hiller von Gaertringen (1895-1904), it seated up to about 1,500 spectators near the agora.

vici:vici-31461.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Astypalaia (Isthmos), Kos's capital until 366 BC, near the acropolis and a temple of Asklepios and Hygieia; the orchestra retains its Dionysus altar (thymele), and the stage building's Doric foundations survive.

vici:vici-31464.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Halasarna near Kardamaina, part of the Sanctuary of Apollo complex; excavated by the Italian Archaeological Mission in 1928, it preserves three cunei of turf-cut seating and a Roman-period stage platform, though nothing is visible today.

vici:vici-31465.desc: Large Roman theatre at Ambavri south of Kos town, about 108 m wide and seating up to 13,000 for events linked to the Temple of Asklepios; built in the 1st century AD and partly excavated by the Italian Mission in 1922 and 1930.

vici:vici-31466.desc: Findspot of Kos's Hellenistic athletics stadium, built in the first half of the 2nd century BC (roughly 180 x 30m, without a curved sphendone); German archaeologist R. Herzog excavated the site in 1899–1900, locating its aphesis (starting line).

vici:vici-31467.desc: Findspot of Kos's Hellenistic athletics stadium (built first half of the 2nd century BC, track roughly 180 x 30m); Italian archaeologist Luciano Laurenzi excavated here in 1928, uncovering rows of travertine seating that confirmed the stadium's identification.

vici:vici-31468.desc: Theatre of the Sanctuary of the Great Gods at Samothrace, built around 200 BC on the Western Hill; it staged mythological performances during the Mysteries, with an orchestra roughly 12.5m across and polychrome seating of red rhyolite and white limestone.

vici:vici-31469.desc: Badly damaged theatre on the north side of Abdera, the Thracian colony founded by settlers from Teos; topographic and geophysical survey (University of Thessaloniki) plus trial trenching have begun defining its plan, though excavation remains at an early stage.

vici:vici-31472.desc: Council house (bouleuterion) of Ariassos, a Pisidian hill city occupied from the 3rd century BC to the 7th century AD; survey by Stephen Mitchell's team identified a squarish hall with a semicircular tiered bench, possibly an odeion, fronted by a hexagonal-facade atrium.

vici:vici-31473.desc: Theatre of Ariassos, a Pisidian city on the Antalya–Burdur road that minted coins in the late Hellenistic period; only partial foundations of the cavea survive, recorded during Stephen Mitchell's Pisidia Survey Project alongside the city's bouleuterion, nymphaeum and baths.

vici:vici-31475.desc: At Sia (also read Osia), a minor Pisidian town near Karaot attested only by a boundary inscription, one surviving structure preserves eight or nine straight rows of stone seating above a paved area, probably a small theatre or council hall.

vici:vici-31476.desc: One of two theatres at Cremna, a Pisidian hilltop city on the Cestrus gorge that Augustus refounded as the Roman colony Colonia Iulia Augusta Felix Cremnena; the city's public buildings flourished from Hadrian's reign into the Severan period.

vici:vici-31477.desc: Theatre of Seleucia Sidera (Claudioseleucia) on Hisar Tepe, built to the Greek plan with a largely destroyed cavea; its stage building dates to the Roman Imperial period, 2nd–3rd century CE.

vici:vici-31478.desc: Theatre of Apamea Kibotos (Cibotus), the Seleucid city Antiochus I founded on the site of Phrygian Celaenae; its remains lie beneath modern Dinar and have seen little systematic excavation.

vici:vici-31479.desc: Theatre-stairway of Pessinus, built beside the sanctuary of Cybele as monumental steps to the Tiberian-era temple, with an orchestra used for religious rites and gladiatorial shows; last major restoration under Hadrian.

vici:vici-31480.desc: Theatre of Soli-Pompeiopolis, the Rhodian colony Pompey refounded in 64 BC to resettle surrendered Cilician pirates; recorded by early 19th-century travelers, it is no longer visible above ground and remains under excavation.

vici:vici-31481.desc: Theatre of Claudiopolis (Colonia Ninica Claudiopolis), a Claudian colony in the Calycadnus valley; seating fragments and scaenae column drums survive west of modern Mut amid an otherwise built-over ancient site.

vici:vici-31482.desc: Theatre of Syedra, a Greek foundation traced to the 7th century BC that flourished under Rome; the roughly 3,000-year-old structure is under active excavation via a Turkish-French research project (TUBITAK-backed).

vici:vici-31484.desc: Stadium of Tripolis on the Meander (Apollonia/Antoniopolis), a city on the Caria-Lydia-Phrygia border; its 2nd-century-CE public buildings, including this stadium, are under ongoing excavation by Pamukkale University.

vici:vici-31487.desc: Theatre of Bargasa, a minor inland Carian town of Hellenistic and Roman date near Haydere/Kavaklı in the Harpasos valley; its ruins are poorly preserved and hidden in woodland.

vici:vici-31491.desc: Theatre of Mastaura, a small Roman-era theatre near Nazilli seating an estimated 1,600, with a partially preserved two-tiered arched stage building; the city also has a recently unearthed Severan-era amphitheater.

vici:vici-31493.desc: Roman-period theatre of Kyon, built against a hillside near Çamyayla village (ancient Paliapolis in Byzantine times); only 13 rows of seating survive and the city, known for its coinage, has never been formally excavated.

vici:vici-31494.desc: Stadium of Philadelphia in Lydia, founded 189 BC by Eumenes II of Pergamon and later one of the Seven Churches of Revelation; the U-shaped cavea survives cut into a hillside, but the structure remains unexcavated and its stone seating stripped.

vici:vici-31495.desc: Theatre of Blaundus, a hilltop city founded by Macedonian settlers under Alexander's successors and rebuilt as a Roman center after earthquakes; the theatre sits on the southern slope outside the walls with its stage destroyed but seating rows partly preserved.

vici:vici-31496.desc: Stadium of Blaundus, a 1st-century AD structure roughly 140 by 37 meters excavated in recent seasons, notable for a stepped entrance and a male statue found reused as filler stone in its wall.

vici:vici-31497.desc: Theatre of Temnos, an Aeolian city mentioned by Herodotus near modern Görece; built on the acropolis' north slope with an estimated capacity of about 2,800, its cavea partly visible but never scientifically excavated.

vici:vici-31498.desc: Theatre of Aigai in Aeolis, a member of the Aeolian dodecapolis and seat of a noted Apollo sanctuary that flourished under the Attalids of Pergamon; the theatre has been under excavation since the site's 2004 research program began.

vici:vici-31499.desc: One long wall survives from the stadium of Aigai (Aeolis), a Greek city atop Gün Dağı in Manisa province; excavated since 2004 by Ege University under Ersin Doğer alongside the city's agora, theater, and Temple of Demeter.

vici:vici-31506.desc: The Roman-Byzantine theater of Abila of the Decapolis (Tell Abil/Quwaylibah) in northern Jordan, part of the ten-city Decapolis league; the site's structures were largely leveled by the earthquake of 747 CE.

vici:vici-31508.desc: Herod the Great built a small theater, seating about 3,000, at Tell es-Samrat north of his winter palace at Jericho; Josephus records the dying Herod ordering Judean notables assembled there executed at his death.

vici:vici-31509.desc: The hippodrome (racecourse) at Tell es-Samrat formed part of Herod the Great's theater-stadium complex north of his winter palace at Jericho, excavated by Ehud Netzer; Josephus sets here Herod's deathbed order to execute assembled notables.

vici:vici-31510.desc: Dor's Roman theater, built in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE north of the tell, was about 72 m in diameter and seated roughly 4,000; partly excavated in the 1950s by Ja'acov Leibowitz.

vici:vici-31515.desc: The only excavated ancient Greek theater in the northern Black Sea region, built in the Hellenistic period within Chersonesus Taurica near Sevastopol; it was later remodeled into an arena for gladiatorial combat under Roman rule.

vici:vici-31516.desc: Ancient sources place a theatre, hippodrome, and gymnasium around Olbia Pontica's agora, but despite more than a century of excavation at this Greek colony on the Southern Bug estuary, the theatre's physical remains have never been located.

vici:vici-31518.desc: Geophysical survey has traced a hippodrome for chariot racing among the public buildings of Viminacium, capital of Roman Moesia Superior near modern Kostolac, though like most of the 450-hectare site it remains unexcavated.

vici:vici-31521.desc: Built into a hillside in the 3rd century BC, this theatre at Nikaia had a 53m cavea seating about 800 in 17 rows; its parodos wall preserves 14 inscriptions recording grants of citizenship to the city.

vici:vici-31522.desc: Built around the mid-3rd century BC, Byllis's roughly 190m stadium ran beside the theatre for standing spectators; a 51m rainwater cistern fed by its steps and the Great Stoa's roof survives alongside one bank of seating.

vici:vici-31523.desc: Plutarch's Life of Timoleon implies a theatre or arena at Messana, where the deposed tyrant Hippon was lynched before the crowd, but no excavation has ever confirmed or located a Hellenistic theatre in the city.

vici:vici-31525.desc: Excavated in 2007 at Montagna dei Cavalli, this Greek theatre — among the earliest known in Sicily — was built in the later 4th century BC and destroyed in 258 BC when Rome sacked Hippana during the First Punic War.

vici:vici-31526.desc: Uncovered in the 1960s on the San Nicola hillock, this 4th–3rd century BC semicircular assembly hall — cut into tufa, seating roughly 3,000 — served Akragas's popular assembly and was later refashioned as a Roman-era odeon called the comitium.

vici:vici-31535.desc: Antioch's amphitheater, built around the mid-1st century BC — one of the earliest in the Roman world — but its exact location was never securely fixed and may be confused in the ancient sources with a nearby reservoir.

vici:vici-31536.desc: Built around 50 BCE on an island in the Orontes, the Circus of Antioch stretched over 490 m and could seat up to 80,000 spectators for chariot racing, among the largest hippodromes in the Roman world.

vici:vici-31606.desc: Archaeological survey has identified traces of a theater among the remains of Neandreia, an Aeolian Greek city on Çığrı Dağ in the Troad founded around 700 BC and largely depopulated after Alexandria Troas absorbed it c. 310 BC.

vici:vici-31610.desc: The Hellenistic-Roman theater of Tripolis ad Maeandrum, near modern Yenicekent, is built into a hillside at a steep 50-degree incline, dates to around the 2nd century AD, and remains largely unexcavated.

vici:vici-31611.desc: The odeon of Pergamon's Upper Gymnasium was a roofed recital hall about 36 m across with 14 rows of seating in five wedges, seating roughly 1,000; built in the 2nd century CE, it may also have served as a bouleuterion.

vici:vici-31612.desc: Pergamon's roofed 'Basement Stadium', about 212 m long, lay between the middle and upper terraces of the 2nd-century-BC gymnasium complex on the acropolis, used for athletic training and competitions.

vici:vici-31632.desc: Tlos's Roman-era stadium, about 148 m long with shops opening onto it, hugs the acropolis slope; excavations since 2005 under Akdeniz University have exposed nine of its original seating rows and an adjoining paved street.

vici:vici-31633.desc: The small Roman-period theater of Nisa, a minor Lycian League city near modern Sütleğen that minted its own coins in the 2nd century BC, has 22 surviving rows of seating and is the best-preserved structure at the unexcavated site.

vici:vici-31846.desc: Hellenistic bouleuterion of Heraclea ad Latmum in Caria, sited at the northeast corner of the agora with U-shaped stone council seating comparable to the bouleuteria at Priene and Miletus; probably 2nd century BC.

vici:vici-32383.desc: Theatre in the Valley of the Muses on Mt Helicon, built for the musical and dramatic contests of the Mouseia festival hosted by Thespiae; dated to the late 3rd–early 2nd century BC, excavated by the French School at Athens in 1888–1890.

vici:vici-32560.desc: Theatre of ancient Elea (Elaea), capital of the Thesprotian koinon from its mid-4th-century BC foundation; the cavea spanned 45–50 m and stood 8–9 m high, seating an estimated 3,000–4,000. Excavation of the site began in 1985.

vici:vici-32922.desc: Minoan 'theatral area' within the Tourkogeitonia palace complex at Epano Archanes: a paved court crossed by raised walkways, with a stepped altar and sacred horns; excavated from 1964 by Yannis Sakellarakis alongside the Bronze Age palace.

vici:vici-33216.desc: The Pi-shaped theatre of Calydon, unusual among Greek theatres for its square orchestra and rectilinear stone benches; found in 1967 during roadworks and fully excavated by a Danish-Greek team 2001-2014, with the stage building dated to the mid-3rd century BC.

vici:vici-34119.desc: Hellenistic-Roman theater of the Sicilian polis Halaesa Archonidea near Tusa; rediscovered in the 2010s and under active excavation since the 2020s by archaeologists from Amiens University with Palermo and Messina, noted for its scale and preservation.

vici:vici-34988.desc: The Odeon of Hippos (Sussita), a Decapolis city above the Sea of Galilee, was a small roofed venue (c. 27 x 21 m, semicircular) for poetry readings and recitals before a privileged audience, uncovered in excavations announced in 2009.

vici:vici-35024.desc: The theatron of Augusta Traiana (Roman Beroe, renamed for Trajan after 106 CE) was built with marble seats under Marcus Aurelius (161-180 CE) for gladiatorial contests, processions, and civic assemblies.

vici:vici-37325.desc: A small Hellenistic theatre survives at Issus (Kinet Höyük, near Yeşilköy, Hatay Province), site of Alexander's decisive 333 BC battle against Darius III; the mound was excavated by Bilkent University between 1992 and 2012.

vici:vici-40183.desc: At Krommyon (modern Agioi Theodoroi) on the Corinthian coast, 1961 excavations uncovered a small circular structure of the late Archaic period (late 6th c. BC); whether it served as a tiny theatral area or a place of worship remains undetermined.

vici:vici-40184.desc: Around 120 CE, under Hadrian's building program, the smaller of Argos's two ancient theatres — a 5th/4th-century BC archaic theatre seating roughly 2,500 — was roofed over and converted into a Roman odeum with brick seating faced in mosaic.

vici:vici-40185.desc: Argos's second, smaller theater, cut into the lower slope near the agora in the 5th century BC and seating around 2,500; under Hadrian the Romans roofed and remodeled it into an odeon for musical and rhetorical performances.

vici:vici-4140.desc: Built at the end of the 3rd century AD within the walls of Diocletianopolis (modern Hisarya, Bulgaria), this irregular pear-shaped amphitheatre seated roughly 3,000 and is among the best-preserved in Bulgaria; its unusually central, intramural location and late Christian-era operation suggest bloodless spectacles.

vici:vici-4147.desc: Built around AD 55-75 outside the walls of Calleva Atrebatum (Silchester), this earthwork amphitheatre held roughly 3,500-7,000 spectators; rebuilt in stone in the 3rd century, it later saw post-Roman British and Anglo-Saxon reuse before an 1147 medieval fortification atop it.

vici:vici-4149.desc: The largest Roman amphitheatre known in Britain, built outside the legionary fortress of Deva Victrix (Chester) by Legio II Adiutrix in the late AD 70s and rebuilt in stone by Legio XX Valeria Victrix around AD 100, seating an estimated 7,000-12,000 for entertainment and military training.

vici:vici-4150.desc: A Neolithic henge at Dorchester (Durnovaria) remodeled by the Romans around AD 60-70 into an amphitheatre, likely by Legio II Augusta, with its banks lowered and an oval arena cut for military displays and public spectacle; later reused as a Civil War fort and 17th-18th century execution site.

vici:vici-4152.desc: An amphitheatre serving Isurium Brigantum (Aldborough), one of Roman Britain's civitas capitals, confirmed only by geomagnetic scanning in 2011; its earthworks were later reused as the motte of a Norman castle on Studforth Hill.

vici:vici-4154.desc: Built c. AD 70-90 outside the walls of Noviomagus Reginorum (Chichester) near Stane Street, this timber-lined amphitheatre measured about 70 by 60 metres; discovered in 1935 by Raymond Carlyon-Britton and excavated by G. M. White, it was then backfilled and is now a shallow depression in a public park.

vici:vici-4158.desc: The amphitheatre of Agedincum (Sens), capital of the Senones in Gallia Lugdunensis; only its foundations survive, buried beneath the modern Rue des Arènes, whose curving course still traces the arena's outline.

vici:vici-4159.desc: The amphitheatre of Aginnum (Agen), built between the Augustan period and the later 1st century AD, measured about 115 x 100 m and held an estimated 12,000-15,000 spectators; its remains were only confirmed by rescue excavations in 1988-1989.

vici:vici-4161.desc: Part of the secondary Gallo-Roman town of Argentomagus (Saint-Marcel, Indre), the amphitheatre stood alongside two successive theatre buildings and was used for hunts, animal displays, and gladiatorial combat.

vici:vici-4166.desc: Known since the Middle Ages as the Palais Gallien, this amphitheatre of Roman Burdigala (Bordeaux) was built in the late 1st or early 2nd century AD, measured 132 x 111 m, and held roughly 22,000 spectators; it is Bordeaux's only visible ancient monument today.

vici:vici-4171.desc: The amphitheatre of Divodurum (Metz), built between the late 1st and mid-2nd century AD, measured about 148 x 124 m with capacity near 25,000 and ranked among the largest in the Roman world; its plan was recovered from remains found in 1902 during railway station construction.

vici:vici-4173.desc: A semi-amphitheatre built into the Mazerolles hillside at Gennes (ancient Geinum) in the late 1st century AD, with a cavea about 94 m across holding an estimated 5,000 spectators; used into the early 3rd century and first reported by antiquarians in 1837.

vici:vici-4174.desc: The amphitheatre at Grand (Andesina), built c. 80-140 AD beside the sanctuary of the healing god Apollo Grannus, had a major axis of 148 m and seated roughly 17,000-20,000, making it one of the ten largest amphitheatres of the Roman world; abandoned in the late 4th century.

vici:vici-4177.desc: One of the grandest amphitheatres of Roman Gaul, built at Limonum (Poitiers) in the 1st-2nd century AD and measuring roughly 156 x 130 m, it may have held 22,000-30,000 spectators and was reportedly refortified as a citadel by the Visigoths in the 5th century.

vici:vici-4183.desc: Unusually built at the very heart of Samarobriva (Amiens) beside the forum rather than on the city's edge, this amphitheatre (built late 1st-mid 2nd century AD, c. 113 x 99 m) could hold up to about 15,000 spectators; its remains lie buried beneath the modern city hall.

vici:vici-4184.desc: Semi-circular theatre-amphitheatre hybrid seating up to 6,600, part of the Gallo-Roman sanctuary of Sanxay, a 1st–2nd-century healing-water cult complex centered on an octagonal temple; excavated by Camille de La Croix from 1881.

vici:vici-4187.desc: Amphitheatre of Roman Vesontio, built in the 1st century AD on the Doubs's far bank with capacity of roughly 20,000, among Gaul's largest; abandoned by the 4th century and later quarried for the citadel and churches of Besançon.

vici:vici-4190.desc: Elliptical amphitheatre (c.100m long) built in 2nd-century AD Gortyn, Roman capital of Crete and Cyrenaica; ten Christians were reportedly killed there by beasts in 249 AD during the Decian persecution.

vici:vici-4204.desc: Amphitheatre of Aquileia, the Roman colony founded 181 BC that grew into a city of some 100,000 with its own circus and port; its remains survive only below ground after the site was sacked by Attila in 452 AD.

vici:vici-4211.desc: Rock-cut amphitheatre of Roman Caralis (Cagliari), built in the 2nd century AD partly carved from the limestone bedrock, seating around 10,000 for gladiatorial combats, beast hunts, and public executions.

vici:vici-4212.desc: Amphitheatre of the Roman colony of Cales, dug into the tuff bedrock and built around the 1st century BC for gladiator shows, distinct from the city's separate stone theatre nearby.

vici:vici-4213.desc: Amphitheatre of Roman Canusium (Canosa di Puglia), an Apulian municipium on the Via Traiana near Trajan's arch; ancient descriptions record it as larger than the amphitheatre at Pompeii.

vici:vici-4220.desc: So-called amphitheatre of Egnatia (ancient Gnathia); its small size (37 x 25 m) and lack of seating tiers or underground chambers have led some scholars to reinterpret it as a market plaza rather than a spectacle building.

vici:vici-4221.desc: The amphitheatre of Roman Eporedia (Ivrea) was built in the mid-1st century AD along the road to Vercelli, an elliptical arena (65m major axis) holding over 10,000 spectators; excavated 1955-1964.

vici:vici-4233.desc: The brick-built amphitheatre of Interamnia Praetuttiorum (Teramo), dated to the late 1st-early 2nd century AD, measured 74 by 56 metres and held roughly 3,500 spectators; its remains were identified in 1937.

vici:vici-4245.desc: The Roman theatre of Neapolis (1st century BC, rebuilt after the AD 62 earthquake or AD 79 eruption), held about 5,000 spectators and is where, per Tacitus and Suetonius, Emperor Nero made his public performing debut.

vici:vici-4251.desc: An amphitheatre at Peltuinum, a Vestinian town on the Via Claudia Nova founded mid-1st century BC and prosperous under Augustus from livestock, wine and saffron trade; the site declined after the earthquake of AD 346.

vici:vici-4252.desc: The amphitheatre of Roman Pollentia (Pollenzo), near the site of the 402 AD Battle of Pollentia where Stilicho defeated Alaric's Visigoths; the structure no longer stands, but the town's circular street plan still traces its outline.

vici:vici-42628.desc: Saittai (Setae), a city of Lydia in the Katakekaumene region visited by Hadrian, is known from four 2nd-3rd century AD gladiator funerary stelae naming fighters Metbysos, Amphiaros, Maternos and Rhodon, alongside a large stadium.

vici:vici-4270.desc: The amphitheatre of Tuder (Todi), built outside the walls in the Augustan-period urban expansion; its remains are partly incorporated into the church of San Nicolò de Cryptis, built inside the old arena.

vici:vici-4273.desc: The Verlasce amphitheatre of Venafrum (Venafro), built in the 1st century AD, measured 110 by 85 metres and is estimated to have held up to 15,000 spectators; an inscription recorded a gens Vibia benefactor's funding of the building.

vici:vici-4289.desc: The most northerly amphitheatre yet found in the Roman Empire, attached to the fort of Trimontium at Newstead; its use was proposed in 1993 and confirmed by a Bradford University excavation in 1996 — an oval arena of 37 by 30m seating about 1,500.

vici:vici-4294.desc: Built under Augustus and dedicated in 8 BC beside the adjoining Roman theatre, Mérida's amphitheatre held about 15,000 spectators for gladiatorial combats and beast hunts; it forms part of the UNESCO-listed Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida.

vici:vici-4296.desc: Oval Roman amphitheatre built for the colonia of veterans Julius Caesar settled behind the older Greek city of Emporion; its 75 by 44m arena held only about 3,300 spectators, a more modest design than originally planned.

vici:vici-4301.desc: Built in the 2nd century AD near Tarraco's forum (130 by 102m, c.15,000 seats), this amphitheatre is where bishop Fructuosus and his deacons Augurius and Eulogius were burned alive in AD 259 during Valerian's persecution of Christians.

vici:vici-4303.desc: Built c. AD 120 and enlarged about 30 years later, the amphitheatre of Aventicum (capital of Roman Helvetia) seated roughly 9,000 across 24 rows; partly quarried for lime in the 6th century, it is Switzerland's best-preserved amphitheatre.

vici:vici-4305.desc: Amphitheatre of the Caesarian veterans' colony Colonia Iulia Equestris (Nyon); discovered by chance in 1996, its arena measured about 50 by 36m and dates to the early 2nd century AD, with an inscription linking construction to the reign of Trajan.

vici:vici-4313.desc: Built in the late 1st or early 2nd century AD with roughly 30,000 seats, one of Roman Africa's largest amphitheatres; traditionally linked to the 203 AD martyrdom of Perpetua and Felicity, commemorated by a 19th-century chapel on the site.

vici:vici-43138.desc: Geophysical survey about 300m east of the Palais des Papes revealed a large circular structure at Roman Avenio (Avignon), interpreted as a theatre or amphitheatre; no remains are visible above ground and its precise function is unconfirmed.

vici:vici-4316.desc: At Roman Limisa (Ksar Lemsa), a small entertainment building — recorded by different sources as an amphitheatre or theatre — stood beside a well-preserved 6th-century Byzantine fortress with intact corner towers; the site was excavated in 1966-69.

vici:vici-4321.desc: At Pheradi Maius (Sidi Khalifa, Sousse Governorate), an African town raised to municipium status under Marcus Aurelius, an amphitheatre stood alongside a triumphal arch, Capitol, nymphaeum, and baths within the town's civic complex.

vici:vici-4327.desc: The theatre at the Gallo-Roman spa sanctuary of Aquae Segetae (Sceaux-du-Gâtinais), dedicated to the healing goddess Segeta, measured about 104m across and could seat over 10,000 pilgrims visiting its baths and temple, peaking in the 2nd century AD.

vici:vici-4330.desc: Thibaris, a town of Africa Proconsularis near ancient galena mines at Djebba, was a Christian bishopric suffragan to Carthage whose bishops attended councils at Carthage in 256 and 411 AD; an amphitheatre also stood among the town's ruins.

vici:vici-4332.desc: At Thimisua (Sidi-Bou-Argoub, Siliana Governorate), a civitas of Africa Proconsularis downstream from Thabbora, the amphitheatre is oval with two opposed entrances set along its longitudinal axis; little else about the structure has been published.

vici:vici-4333.desc: At Thugga (Dougga), Tunisia's best-preserved small Roman town, an amphitheatre stands apart from the site's famous 3,500-seat theatre, built in 168-169 AD by local benefactor P. Marcius Quadratus; the amphitheatre itself is far less studied.

vici:vici-4341.desc: One of two Roman amphitheatres identified at Utica, the Phoenician-founded city that became capital of Africa Proconsularis after Carthage's destruction in 146 BC; today largely ruined and unexcavated.

vici:vici-4343.desc: Part of Aizanoi's unique theatre-stadium complex in Phrygia (2nd-3rd c. AD): the theatre and stadium stand directly adjoined, separated only by a colonnaded street, with capacities of roughly 20,000 and 13,500 respectively.

vici:vici-4345.desc: Built around AD 80 for Legion II Augusta's fortress at Caerleon; timber benches seated up to 6,000 for gladiatorial games. Excavated in 1926-27, it is Britain's most complete Roman amphitheatre, long known locally as 'King Arthur's Round Table'.

vici:vici-43615.desc: Amphitheatre of Obulco, rediscovered in 2014 beneath Porcuna's Hoyo Mendo district and excavated from 2022; built c. 1st century BC/AD for a city Caesar used as a base before the Battle of Munda (45 BC), holding over 10,000.

vici:vici-43776.desc: Attalea (modern Antalya) was founded as a naval base by King Attalus II of Pergamon, probably before 150 BC, and named for him; bequeathed to Rome in 133 BC by Attalus III.

vici:vici-4390.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre on Mont-Auxois, built in the later 1st century AD within the vicus that grew on the site of Caesar's siege of Alesia (52 BC); semicircular cavea about 81m across, seating an estimated 5,000.

vici:vici-44812.desc: Theatre of Aregenua, capital of the Viducasses near Caen, built as the town acquired monumental Roman-style buildings in the 1st-3rd centuries AD; its cavea measured about 80 by 67 metres, a mid-sized Gallo-Roman theatre.

vici:vici-44923.desc: The Thersilion, council hall of the Arcadian League built c. 370 BC beside the theatre of Megalopolis and nicknamed the 'Hall of the Ten Thousand'; destroyed by Cleomenes III of Sparta in 222 BC.

vici:vici-45466.desc: Detected by georadar and LiDAR survey in 2016 within the walled Iberian-Roman city of Castulo (Linares, Jaén), one of several monumental buildings — alongside a theatre, forum and baths — from the city's Roman-era expansion; largely unexcavated.

vici:vici-45510.desc: Its remains lie beneath Écija's 19th-century bullring; at roughly 130 x 107 m with an 80-arch exterior facade modeled on Rome's Flavian Amphitheatre, it likely shared architects with the amphitheatre at nearby Italica.

vici:vici-45568.desc: Unlike the city's well-preserved Roman theatre, the amphitheatre of Caesaraugusta has never been conclusively located; a long-standing theory placing it beneath the Hotel Palafox site has not been confirmed by excavation.

vici:vici-46394.desc: Missua (modern Sidi Daoud, Cap Bon) was a Roman port town of Africa Proconsularis with trade ties to Ostia and a Christian bishopric attested from 484 AD; its theatre remains stand near the ancient harbor and breakwater.

vici:vici-48059.desc: First identified via 2016 LiDAR and georadar survey north of Castulo's walled Iberian-Roman city (Linares, Jaén); its existence had long been suspected but its exact location was only pinpointed through this remote-sensing work.

vici:vici-48683.desc: A largely vanished Roman amphitheatre of Barcino; no confirmed remains have been excavated, though archaeologist Jordina Sales hypothesizes a location near Santa Maria del Mar based on the district's oval street pattern.

vici:vici-49118.desc: A circus at the Roman municipium of Singilia Barba, identified through epigraphic evidence and geophysical survey near the city's buried theatre (capacity c. 2,200), part of a monumental zone that also included a forum.

vici:vici-49794.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre of Iatinum (Meaux), built in the 1st–2nd century AD; a 1993–94 rescue excavation uncovered cavea substructures spanning over 150 m, and an inscription credits its funding to a local notable, Orgetorix.

vici:vici-49922.desc: Gallo-Roman amphitheatre of Vindunum (Le Mans), capital of the Aulerci Cenomani; rediscovered in 1791 by architect Daudin during urban works and destroyed the following year when the Jacobins promenade was laid out.

vici:vici-50043.desc: Structure at the Roman town of Contributa Iulia Ugultunia (Medina de las Torres, Badajoz), identified in 2016 via geophysical survey as an amphitheatre, one of the first found this way in Iberia; the site preserves an unusually intact street grid.

vici:vici-50056.desc: Circus of the Roman colony Augusta Firma Astigi (Écija), built outside the walls between the Via Augusta and the road to Augusta Emerita; a semicircular structure near the presumed prima meta was clarified by 2023–2024 excavations.

vici:vici-50062.desc: Roman circus of ancient Consabura, confirmed by geophysical survey and 2017 excavations; an 18th-century plan recorded it at roughly 370 x 80 m with a capacity near 10,000, one of only about a dozen archaeologically documented circuses in Roman Hispania.

vici:vici-50599.desc: Small horseshoe-shaped Nabataean-Roman theater (39 m wide, 11 rows seating 500-800) cut into a gully at Sabra near Petra; built in the 1st century AD with a water-channel system beneath the seating, remaining in use into the late Roman period.

vici:vici-50768.desc: The unfinished Roman theater of Palmyra, built in the Severan 2nd century AD with a cavea of eleven sections/twelve rows never completed; buried in sand for centuries, it was excavated and restored in the 1950s, and its stage facade was destroyed by ISIL in 2017.

vici:vici-52623.desc: Built in the early 4th century AD under the Tetrarchy, this roughly 450 x 95 m racecourse was the site of the 390 AD massacre in which Theodosius I's Gothic troops killed thousands of citizens after a riot; fragments survive beneath the modern Hippodromiou district.

vici:vici-54949.desc: Geophysical surveys announced in 2023 revealed likely remains of a Roman circus east of Italica's Hadrianic quarter — an exceptionally large structure some 532 m long with capacity for up to 80,000 spectators; a 1799 mosaic of chariot-race scenes and racehorse graffiti in the city's theater had earlier hinted at its existence.

vici:vici-55079.desc: Built around AD 15–45, among the earliest amphitheaters raised in Roman Gaul alongside those of Lyon and Saintes, it stood north of Durocortorum near the road to Boulogne-sur-Mer; its remains had vanished by the mid-19th century and were rediscovered in 2007–08 during tramway construction.

vici:vici-55148.desc: The colonnaded porticus post scaenam behind Leptis Magna's theater enclosed a temple to the Dei Augusti; built by Iddibal of the Tapapius family (who also built the theater itself) and dedicated by the proconsul Quintus Marcius Barea.

vici:vici-55892.desc: The council house of Hellenistic Sia, a Pisidian city near Bucak founded c. 2nd century BC; it is the only building at the site securely predating Roman rule, when three temples, an agora with Doric stoa, and Byzantine basilicas were added.

vici:vici-55971.desc: Amphitheatre (about 80 by 45 m) of the Roman colony of Sinuessa, founded 296 BC near modern Mondragone; the town was noted for its thermal springs and Falernian wine before coastal erosion later submerged part of the settlement.

vici:vici-57005.desc: Stadium of ancient Sicyon, lying just west of the city's theatre and shaped largely by the hillside; unlike fully excavated Greek stadia, seating did not run the full length of the track, with one end built up on fill.

vici:vici-58188.desc: Athens' Panathenaic Stadium was built c. 330 BC under Lycurgus for the Panathenaic Games, rebuilt entirely in Pentelic marble by Herodes Atticus by 144 AD, and restored again for the first modern Olympics in 1896 as the world's only all-marble stadium.

vici:vici-58426.desc: Amphitheatre of Trea, a Roman municipium on a Picene/Sabine hilltop near modern Treia that flourished from Augustus to Antoninus Pius; its oval outline is still traceable as a crop mark near the Santuario del Santissimo Crocifisso.

vici:vici-58447.desc: Geophysical survey in 2010–11 near the Roman fort Iciniacum (Theilenhofen) on the Raetian Limes revealed a c. 75 m theatre-shaped structure beside a large plaza interpreted as a forum, probably temporary buildings for a single major event.

vici:vici-58541.desc: A curving timber structure identified as a possible amphitheatre stood in the civilian settlement outside the east rampart of Inveresk Roman fort near Musselburgh, occupied from Antoninus Pius's AD 139/140 invasion; if confirmed, the empire's northernmost amphitheatre.

vici:vici-58553.desc: A possible amphitheatre with only scant surface traces, lying east of the mid-1st-century Roman auxiliary fort at Forden Gaer, which guarded a crossing of the River Severn near Montgomery, Wales.

vici:vici-58567.desc: Known only from inscriptions (CIL XIII 4132, 198 CE, and a text of 253 CE) recording that Lucius Ammatius Gamburio endowed the theatre of Beda (Bitburg) with 50,000 denarii and funded annual games, probably for the Floralia; the building itself has never been located.

vici:vici-58763.desc: Amphitheatre of Roman Hatria (Adria); its buried foundations near the Canalbianco were found in rescue excavations and later traced by ground-penetrating radar, its superstructure having been stripped away in the Middle Ages.

vici:vici-59657.desc: Site of a Roman fortified camp at Liffremont; 19th-century finds included gold, silver and bronze imperial coins and the 3rd-century marble 'Venus of Liffremont' (now in Rouen's Museum of Antiquities); the location of a theatre was also identified here, though it remains unexcavated.

vici:vici-60167.desc: Small theatre in Theveste (Tébessa, Algeria), built after the city was refounded as a base for Legio III Augusta; the colony also has a 3rd-century triumphal arch of Caracalla and a 4th-century amphitheatre.

vici:vici-60175.desc: 1st-century AD theatre of the Roman town of Libarna (Piedmont), with a 22-arch external ambulatory on sandstone-based pillars; the ruins, later damaged by farming and two railway lines, sit beside the town's amphitheatre and baths.

vici:vici-60254.desc: Josephus records that Herod the Great funded a theatre at Damascus, one of several benefactions (gymnasia, temples, colonnades) to Greek cities outside his own kingdom; the structure's physical remains have not been securely located.
