vici:vici-60356.desc: Not a built amphitheatre but a natural rock formation ('Pietrele Scrise') near Casian in Dobrogea, bearing two Greek boundary inscriptions ('Oroi Kasianon') dated to the 2nd-3rd century AD and first deciphered by Vasile Pârvan in 1912-13.

vici:vici-61475.desc: Hypothesized Roman circus at Nemausus (Nîmes), proposed by archaeologist Richard Pellé from imported sand deposits found near Rue Cité Foulc matching the arena floor; if real, it would postdate the amphitheatre, built in Beaucaire limestone in the later 2nd century AD.

vici:vici-61640.desc: 2nd-century AD amphitheatre at the 'Villa of the Antonines' near Genzano di Roma (ancient Lanuvium), a family estate of the Antonine dynasty; possibly the arena where Commodus killed wild beasts and earned the nickname 'Roman Hercules'.

vici:vici-61935.desc: A Gallo-Roman theatre discovered in 1993 near the sanctuary of Blicquy, the only known Roman theatre building in Belgium; timber-and-stone construction about 70m across, estimated to have seated 5,000-6,000.

vici:vici-62026.desc: Theatre of the Illyrian hill city of Dimale (Dimallum), built amid Hellenistic urban expansion in the 3rd-2nd century BC; the fortified city was captured by Rome under L. Aemilius Paullus in 219 BC after a seven-day siege.

vici:vici-62184.desc: A hippodrome tentatively identified in 1993 from Roman-period brick pillars at Pelusium (Tell el-Farama), one of only two hippodromes known from Roman Egypt, alongside Antinoöpolis.

vici:vici-62185.desc: A large imperial-period amphitheatre at Pelusium (Tell el-Farama), excavated from 1992 and linked to a drainage system serving the surrounding city; identified by archaeologist Ahmed el-Tabai.

vici:vici-62189.desc: A modest wooden amphitheatre with a circular palisade ditch built in the southern corner of the timber-and-earth Roman fort at Unterkirchberg (Illerkirchberg) on the Raetian Danube limes, founded under Claudius.

vici:vici-62216.desc: The Roman circus of Carteia (San Roque, Cadiz), whose spina and meta secunda were documented in 2017 excavations, confirming a chariot-racing track at this Phoenician-Carthaginian-Roman port city on the Bay of Gibraltar.

vici:vici-62218.desc: The Roman circus of Valentia (Valencia), built in the 2nd century AD, measured roughly 350 x 70m and could seat up to 10,000; first identified in 1987, its remains lie beneath several buildings in Ciutat Vella.

vici:vici-62225.desc: Site of Roman Consabura, predecessor of Consuegra; a rare Roman circus — one of only about a dozen securely attested in Hispania — was excavated here in December 2017 near Viriato Park.

vici:vici-62499.desc: Oval Roman amphitheatre of Divona Cadurcorum (Cahors), c. 110 by 90 metres, built in the 1st century AD; its buried remains were found beneath Place Gambetta during car-park construction and opened to visitors in 2009.

vici:vici-62501.desc: The Roman theatre of Augustodunum (Autun), built into a hillside in the later 1st century AD, spanned some 148 metres and held up to c. 14,000 — the second-largest theatre in the Roman world after Pompey's in Rome.

vici:vici-62995.desc: Located within the Hellenistic-Roman city of Zeugma on the Euphrates, a Seleucid foundation that became a Roman legionary base and major commercial hub; the stadium is among the site's identified but still largely unexcavated public buildings.

vici:vici-63003.desc: Small odeon at Pompeiopolis, the Paphlagonian capital founded by Pompey the Great in 64 BCE (modern Taşköprü); German-led excavations resumed here in 2024 and exposed the structure beneath a roughly two-metre-thick ash layer.

vici:vici-63321.desc: Roman-era remains found in 1908 on Pisa's Via San Zeno during foundations work for the Institute of Physiology; a contemporary survey (kept at the Museo di San Matteo) never resolved whether the structure was a theatre or an amphitheatre, and it remains unexcavated, buried beneath the modern city.

vici:vici-63461.desc: Roman theatre at the Emmatha hot springs (Hamat Gader), built in the 3rd century CE with about 2,000 basalt seats arranged in 15 rows around the orchestra, near baths begun by the Legio X Fretensis garrison from Gadara.

vici:vici-63475.desc: The southern end of Herod's hippodrome/stadium at Caesarea (built 10/9 BC, c.457x76m, seating ~38,000) was rebuilt in the 2nd century CE into an oval amphitheater for gladiatorial combat and venationes.

vici:vici-63557.desc: The Great Theatre of Roman Ilium (Troy), first built c.306 BC, was rebuilt under Augustus with a three-tiered scaenae frons (completed 12-11 BC); cavea diameter 98.6m, seating roughly 7,700-9,650.

vici:vici-63803.desc: A medium-sized (c.70x100m) amphitheater of Forum Sempronii, identified in 2009 aerial photography near modern Fossombrone; its rubble-core walls faced in opus vittatum remain largely buried and unexcavated.

vici:vici-64584.desc: Despite the generic vici label "Circus," this point marks the Roman theatre of Alba Fucens, built 2nd-1st century BC on the Pettorino hill slope east of the Hercules sanctuary; no longer visible above ground today.

vici:vici-64631.desc: Greek Calatis (Heraclea Pontica colony, founded 6th c. BC; modern Mangalia, Romania) is attested by inscriptions as having had a theatre, but the building's physical remains have never been located archaeologically.

vici:vici-64633.desc: A late 2nd-century CE inscription honoring citizen Marcus Manlius Modestus Quietianus records his private funding of a theatre at Curubis (modern Korba, Tunisia); the building itself has never been located.

vici:vici-64635.desc: Theater of the Romano-Berber town Furnos Minus, identified with Henchir-el-Msaadine near Tebourba; a bishopric of Africa Proconsularis whose Christian cemetery yielded a celebrated 5th–6th-century mosaic of Daniel in the Lions' Den, now in Tunisia's Bardo Museum.

vici:vici-64636.desc: Theater of Gurza, a civitas of Roman Byzacena at modern Kalâa Kebira and later seat of an episcopal see (revived in 1933 as a Catholic titular bishopric); the theater is catalogued in Lachaux's survey of Proconsular African theaters.

vici:vici-64639.desc: Theater of Vallitanus (Sidi Medien), a Romano-Berber colonia and Africa Proconsularis bishopric under Carthage; the theater ruins there are documented in Frank Sear's architectural survey of Roman theaters, alongside inscriptions naming local officials.

vici:vici-64641.desc: Roman theater of Kissamos (Kastelli Kissamou), the free Roman city (civitas libera) that succeeded Hellenistic Kisamos as Polyrrhenia's port; first noted by 16th-century observer Onorio Belli, its seating and stage-building stones mapped by Thomas Spratt in the 19th century are now lost.

vici:vici-64642.desc: Theater of Antinoöpolis, the city Hadrian founded on 30 October AD 130 on the Nile in memory of his drowned companion Antinous; its ruins near the southern gate, still visible to Napoleonic surveyors and recorded in the Description de l'Égypte, were later quarried away for building stone.

vici:vici-64643.desc: Theater of Crocodilopolis/Arsinoe (Medinet el-Fayum), the Ptolemaic-Roman capital of the Faiyum and cult center of the crocodile god Sobek, whose sacred pool-kept crocodile Petsuchos, adorned with gold pendants, drew pilgrims and tourists into Roman times.

vici:vici-64645.desc: A vast 2nd-century AD Roman theatre excavated by Flinders Petrie, estimated to have seated up to 11,000 spectators — among the largest in Roman Egypt; later stripped as a stone quarry, leaving only fragmentary remains.

vici:vici-64647.desc: The chronicler John Malalas records that Vespasian built this theatre on the site of a former synagogue using spoils from the Jewish War; American and French teams excavated it in 1932-1940.

vici:vici-64648.desc: Roman-era theatre of Seleucia ad Calycadnum (modern Silifke); largely unexcavated, overgrown and built over, with its orchestra long used as a depot for local buses.

vici:vici-64650.desc: A theatre has long been conjectured for Hellenistic-Roman Kition (Larnaca) but has never been definitively located or excavated; most of the ancient city lies buried beneath the modern town.

vici:vici-64653.desc: This 2nd-century Roman amphitheatre of Narbo Martius measured about 122 x 93 m and could hold an estimated 25,000 spectators; its buried remains, located via 1838 surveys, underlie modern Narbonne's street plan.

vici:vici-64666.desc: Amphitheater of the Roman spa town Aquae Statiellae (modern Acqui Terme), built on the settlement's outskirts near the Via Aemilia Scauri and identified by excavation in the 1960s alongside the town's thermal baths.

vici:vici-65356.desc: Amphitheater of Roman Carpis (colonia Iulia Carpitana), a Cape Bon coastal town possibly founded under Caesar or Augustus; its rubble-masonry remains stand beside a large cistern that received an aqueduct at El Mraissa.

vici:vici-65977.desc: Amphitheater of Roman Parma, known as the 'Arena' in ancient and medieval sources; sections of its perimeter wall found in 1846 and 1933 suggest a capacity near 25,000 seated spectators before its destruction in the 6th century.

vici:vici-66000.desc: Ancient Panormus is attested to have had a circus and amphitheater by a 2nd-century AD inscription honoring one Aurelianus for staging games; aerial-photo analysis has proposed a curving structure in the Loggia/Vucciria quarter, though the site remains debated.

vici:vici-66001.desc: Panormus's amphitheater is attested by a 2nd-century AD inscription from San Cataldo recording gladiatorial games and animal hunts sponsored by one Aurelianus; its exact location is debated, with this Loggia/Vucciria-quarter site suggested by aerial-photo curvature analysis.

vici:vici-66293.desc: Roman amphitheater of Augustodunum (Autun), built in the second half of the 1st century AD next to the city's famous theater; at roughly 154x130m it ranked among the largest in the Roman Empire, but was fully quarried away by the 18th century and rediscovered only through 1832 and 1844 excavations.

vici:vici-66294.desc: The Grohan amphitheater of Juliomagus (Angers), built around AD 115 with capacity for roughly 6,000 spectators; unusually well-documented from a 1637 survey by Claude Ménard, it survived until the 17th century before being destroyed, with the last traces lost to 19th-century street works.

vici:vici-66413.desc: Nevers occupies the site of Noviodunum, an Aeduan oppidum on the Loire where Caesar stockpiled Gallic hostages, grain, army pay and cavalry horses in 52 BC; the Aedui massacred the Roman garrison there and burned the town during the revolt recounted in his Gallic War, book VII. No specific theater structure at the site is confirmed by excavation.

vici:vici-66417.desc: Remains of a spectacle building at Aquae Sextiae (Aix-en-Provence), discovered only in 2004; the curvature of its excavated seating rows has not settled whether it was a semicircular theater or an oval amphitheater.

vici:vici-66446.desc: Roman theater of Tolosa (Toulouse), built under Augustus between the Place du Pont-Neuf and La Daurade with seating for about 8,000; its foundations were located in 1869 during construction of rue de Metz, but virtually nothing survives above ground today.

vici:vici-66499.desc: Square Castan in Besançon (Roman Vesontio): a semicircular hemicycle roughly 54m across, ringed by eight re-erected Corinthian columns, uncovered by Auguste Castan in 1870. Long identified as a Roman theater, specialists now favor reading it as a colonnaded podium of uncertain function tied to the Arcier aqueduct's water-distribution basin.

vici:vici-66536.desc: The Great Theatre of ancient Ambracia, discovered in 1981 near Arta's Tsakalov Street; the Corinthian colony became King Pyrrhus of Epirus's capital in 295 BC, who adorned it with theatres and palaces.

vici:vici-67575.desc: The stadium of Antioch in Pisidia (near Yalvaç), a Hellenistic-period structure restored in the 2nd century AD and later dismantled by Christians in late antiquity, served the Roman colony founded by Augustus in 25 BC.

vici:vici-67867.desc: The Hellenistic odeon of Selinus in Cilicia, cut into a rectangular cavea seating roughly 1,000, stands near the spot where Emperor Trajan died on 8 August 117 AD, after which the town was briefly renamed Trajanopolis.

vici:vici-67883.desc: A stadium associated with the fortified Roman/Byzantine city of Lamos in Rough Cilicia, whose surviving gate bears a dedication to Emperor Gallienus; most standing remains date to the 2nd-3rd centuries AD.

vici:vici-68504.desc: The theatre of Mopsuestia (Misis) on the Pyramus (Ceyhan) river, a Cilician city legendarily founded by the seer Mopsus, whose surviving ruins also include a Roman bridge, stadium and colonnaded street.

vici:vici-68517.desc: The stadium of Apamea Kibotos (Celaenae) in Phrygia, founded by Antiochus I Soter and site of the 188 BC Treaty of Apamea between Rome and the Seleucids; partial remains survive near modern Dinar.

vici:vici-69769.desc: Theatre of Ruessium, Gallo-Roman capital of the Vellavi civitas, apparently built around AD 50 under Claudius; its site was recently proposed via archival and cadastral research, comparable in scale to the theatres of Moingt and Javols.

vici:vici-69855.desc: Theatre of Hypaepa, a Lydian city in the Cayster valley regarded as second only to Ephesus in regional importance; the theatre stood on the western slopes of the acropolis within the fortified city.

vici:vici-70239.desc: Remains of Corinth's Roman circus, identified as the 'Apsidal Building' uncovered in the 1967-68 Gymnasium-area excavations; planned into the Caesarian city's Augustan design, it hosted equestrian events of the Caesarea and Isthmian games.

vici:vici-70249.desc: Roman circus of Carmona, an estimated 290 m long and built into the local slope, part of the city's spectacle complex with its amphitheatre and necropolis; poorly documented and its construction date still uncertain.

vici:vici-70582.desc: Roman circus of Corduba (Cordoba), capital of Baetica, built between the reigns of Nero and Domitian at roughly 400 x 100 m with an ornamented spina; abandoned after structural failure by the mid-2nd century AD.

vici:vici-70674.desc: Open equestrian training ground in the Campus Martius, named for the triga (three-horse chariot); first attested under Claudius and used into the 4th century, kept free of permanent structures near the Tiber bend.

vici:vici-70699.desc: Theatre of Artaxata, the Hellenistic Artaxiad capital founded c. 176 BC on the Araxes; excavations have identified it as Armenia's earliest known theatre, evidence of the city's Greek-influenced civic and cultural life.

vici:vici-71499.desc: Inscribed stone seating steps from the Roman theatre of Lopodunum (Ladenburg), found in the Burgacker district and identified in the 1960s by archaeologist Berndmark Heukemes; donor inscriptions name citizens such as Optatius Tetricius.

vici:vici-71652.desc: The Roman theatre of Ostra, excavated in 1903–04 and restored in 2020, measured only 44.6 m across with three tiers of seating — the smallest Roman theatre known in the Marche region.

vici:vici-71807.desc: Theatre remains within Potentia, a Roman colony founded 184 BC on the Adriatic coast to settle Punic War veterans; the town prospered on the terracotta trade before a 56 BC earthquake devastated it.

vici:vici-73115.desc: The Gallo-Roman theatre of Neung-sur-Beuvron, dated late 1st–early 2nd century AD, formed part of a rural sanctuary with a temple and spring; c. 100 m across, it held perhaps 7,000 spectators and was used until the 3rd century.

vici:vici-73117.desc: A Gallo-Roman theatre discovered in 1834 near the probable site of Noviodunum of the Bituriges, but never excavated or documented since.

vici:vici-73275.desc: The Roman theatre of Antipolis is attested chiefly by the funerary stele of the child performer Septentrion, who ‘danced and was applauded’ on its stage; sited near the Porte Royale, it was demolished in 1691.

vici:vici-73276.desc: A Roman amphitheatre at Antipolis is known only from its oval outline on 17th–18th-century fortification plans of Antibes; its physical remains vanished during the town's refortification, and scholars still debate whether it truly existed.

vici:vici-73280.desc: An early Hellenistic theatre unearthed in 2016 by archaeologist Xeni Arapogianni on the Ellinika ridge at Thouria, near Kalamata; its 16.3 m limestone orchestra and first row of seats survive from when Thouria was the leading city of western Messenia.

vici:vici-73281.desc: A Roman-era theatre with orchestra, stone-backed seating and a niche was found in 2006 beside a Roman mausoleum and cemetery at Fiskardo (ancient Panormos), Kefalonia, during hotel construction.

vici:vici-73330.desc: Roman-period theatre at Savatra (Soatra), an autonomous Lycaonian city near modern Yağlıbayat, Turkey; excavations from 2021 exposed its stage, orchestra and cavea steps — a rare central-Anatolian example, said to have doubled as the city's council hall.

vici:vici-73352.desc: Earthen stadium in the sanctuary quarter of Dion, Macedonia, tied to the "Olympia" games traditionally founded under Archelaus I (late 5th c. BC); excavated in 1995, with coin finds spanning Alexander I to Philip V.

vici:vici-73353.desc: A ~32m stadium with three rows of stone seating, the only one known in western mainland Greece besides Olympia's, found in 2014 in the sanctuary of Athena and Poseidon on the acropolis of ancient Molykreion, Aetolia.

vici:vici-73358.desc: U-shaped horse- and chariot-racing course on Delos (west side ~300m long), edged by a terrace wall built for Athens' 5th-century BC revival of the games, with a horse-watering well near the presumed starting line.

vici:vici-73370.desc: Stadium of Megalopolis, capital of the Arcadian League founded 371–368 BC; Pausanias records a temple of Dionysus on its east side and a spring sacred to the god within it, adjoining the city's colossal theatre.

vici:vici-73795.desc: Multi-period site on the River Wharfe with two successive Roman forts, temporary camps and a vicus guarding the Lincoln–Aldborough road crossing; no securely attested Roman theatre survives, though the adjacent Neolithic henge has been speculated to have been reused as an amphitheatre.

vici:vici-74491.desc: Seleucid city (earlier Pythopolis), founded/enlarged by Antiochus I at the Maeander–Orsinus confluence in Caria; ancient sources attest both a theatre and a stadium, of which a stone Roman-period stadium ~200m long survives near modern Kuyucak.

vici:vici-78057.desc: Hellenistic-era theatre of ancient Syros; its ashlar seating blocks survive incorporated into later buildings near Ano Syra, one of the few traces of the island's ancient public architecture.

vici:vici-78059.desc: Theatre of Pagai (Pagae), a fortified Megarian harbour city on the Corinthian Gulf near modern Kato Alepochori, strategically important from the Persian Wars through the Peloponnesian War as recorded by Thucydides.

vici:vici-7892.desc: Small Roman odeon seating about 500, built in the 2nd century CE (likely under Antoninus Pius or Marcus Aurelius) beside the larger Roman Theatre of Philadelphia; used for music, poetry, and lectures.

vici:vici-78993.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre uncovered in a 2022 rescue excavation near Metz, on the Roman road to Reims via Verdun; estimated to have held up to 6,000 spectators, possibly at the site of ancient Ibliodurum.

vici:vici-7905.desc: Roman theatre built under Augustus as part of Glanum's monumental rebuilding alongside temples, a basilica, baths, and a triumphal arch, on a Hellenistic town site centered on a sacred healing spring.

vici:vici-80853.desc: Chariot-racing circus at Hadrumetum (Sousse); its existence is attested chiefly by 3rd-century AD mosaics, including one from the House of Sorothus naming winning circus horses such as Adorandus and Crinitus.

vici:vici-80875.desc: Roman hippodrome of Caesarea Mazaca (Kayseri), capital of Roman Cappadocia from Tiberius's reign; buried beneath the modern city, its roughly 450 m track was located in 2024 via old maps and geophysical survey.

vici:vici-81143.desc: The imperial hippodrome of Sirmium, built c. 312–324 under Licinius or Constantine beside the tetrarchic imperial palace; roughly 450 x 150 m and seating up to 10,000, it is the only known unexcavated Roman hippodrome, lying buried beneath modern Sremska Mitrovica.

vici:vici-8145.desc: One of a rare pair of earthen amphitheatres in the vicus of the Flavian-era auxiliary fort Zugmantel on the Upper Germanic Limes; an oval roughly 50 m across ringed by a 13 m-wide, 1.5 m-high earth bank, likely combining spectacle with troop training ground.

vici:vici-8146.desc: The second of two earthen amphitheatres flanking the civilian settlement of the Roman fort Zugmantel on the Upper Germanic Limes — amphitheatres are very rare on the frontier, and this pair likely served both as performance venues and as training grounds for the garrison.

vici:vici-81558.desc: Theatre of Roman Augusta in Cilicia, a colony founded in 20 AD and named for Livia; a 1955 rescue survey recorded its open-air auditorium (c. 62 m across) along with a triumphal arch, colonnaded streets, and baths just before the site was submerged by the new Seyhan Dam reservoir.

vici:vici-82733.desc: Site of Troesmis, a Roman legionary fortress on the Lower Danube limes, home of Legio V Macedonica (107–163) and later a municipium under Marcus Aurelius (c. 177); no amphitheatre here is independently confirmed in the literature.

vici:vici-82904.desc: Aquae Flaviae, a thermal spa town in Roman Gallaecia named for the Flavian dynasty, raised to municipium status in 79 CE under Vespasian; famed for its hot springs and later spanned by Trajan's bridge over the Tâmega.

vici:vici-83086.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre-amphitheatre hybrid in Lutetia (Paris), built in the 1st century CE and seating up to 15,000; sacked in the 275 raids, later filled in, then rediscovered by Théodore Vacquer in the 1860s and saved after Victor Hugo's campaign.

vici:vici-83134.desc: Hradisko hilltop fortress near Mušov, the principal Roman military base of the Marcomannic Wars (172–180), built deep in Germanic territory under Marcus Aurelius; excavations found a bath complex, but no theatre or amphitheatre is attested at the site.

vici:vici-84196.desc: Cadyanda's Hellenistic theatre, built late 2nd–early 1st century BCE, was repaired and reused under Roman rule; about 20 rows of seating survive on the hillside above Üzümlü, though the stage building has been destroyed.

vici:vici-84198.desc: Theater identified in 1971 in Vibo Valentia's Terravecchia quarter (ancient Hipponion, a Greek colony of Locri Epizefiri, refounded as the Latin colony Valentia in 192 BC); only retaining walls and part of the lower cavea's steps survive.

vici:vici-84203.desc: Part of the Sanctuary of Diana Nemorensis at Lake Nemi (nemus Aricinum), founded c. 300 BC around the cult of Diana of the Wood; the complex also had baths, a nymphaeum and pilgrim lodgings for the annual August Nemoralia festival.

vici:vici-84206.desc: Theater of the Roman colony Minervia Scolacium, founded 123-122 BC by Gaius Gracchus on the Greek site of Skylletion; built into the hillside with room for c. 3,500 spectators, it flourished under Nerva's refoundation around AD 96.

vici:vici-84213.desc: An AD 148 inscription from the hilltop town of Mons Feretrius (modern San Leo) records a marble-adorned theatre — "theatrum marmoribus ornatum" — the principal evidence for the building, whose remains are not extensively excavated.

vici:vici-84214.desc: The Roman theatre of Pisaurum (Pesaro) lies beneath the former Filanda silk-mill on Via De Amicis; excavations resumed there in 2021 under the Marche Superintendency as part of plans for a future museum of Roman Pesaro.

vici:vici-84215.desc: The theatre of Pitinum Mergens, identified near Catirano along the Via Flaminia by University of Urbino excavations, had a stage set parallel to the road and two concentric corridors supporting a cavea about 70 m in diameter.

vici:vici-84216.desc: Built in the Augustan era on the slope of Genoa's San Giusto hill overlooking the harbor and restored under Trajan by Q. Petronius Modestus, the theatre stayed in use into late antiquity (even hosting gladiatorial shows); identified in 1814, excavated in 1938.

vici:vici-84217.desc: Theatre of Roman Pollentia (Pollenzo), built in the earlier 1st century AD with a two-tier cavea about 74 m across and a 34 m orchestra, seating roughly 6,000 — the largest Roman theatre known in Piedmont.

vici:vici-84220.desc: Roman theatre of Laus Pompeia (Lodi Vecchio): so little structural fabric survives along the presumed main street that scholars could attempt only a virtual reconstruction of its stage building.

vici:vici-84222.desc: Per Diodorus Siculus, a native of Agyrium, its theatre — part of Timoleon's building program after 339 BC, when he settled 10,000 Greek colonists there — ranked as the finest in Sicily after Syracuse's; little survives beneath modern Agira.
